index.title: index
index.content:
Index
Content catalog of the wiki. Grouped by category. Each page: wikilink +
one-line summary. Read this first when answering a query, then drill into
the relevant pages. Updated on every ingest.
Overview

overview — the evolving top-level thesis

Topics

splicing (topic hub) — pre-mRNA / alternative splicing; curated collection of 7 sources + concept map

Sources (9)

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — spliceosome biochemistry & structure; the spliceosome as a ribozyme; mechanisms of AS regulation (foundational, Annu Rev Biochem 2015)
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — physiology of alternative splicing; isoform diversity in tissues, thermal regulation, cancer, ASD (NRMCB 2022)
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — alternative splicing as an evolutionarily labile source of phenotypic diversity (NRG 2022)
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — regulation mechanisms + disease + first splicing-modulating therapies (NRG 2022)
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — the splicing code: computational models from PWMs to deep learning (NRG 2024)
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — post-transcriptional splicing; splicing timing as a regulatory layer (NRG 2025)
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — framing editorial / orientation to the splicing collection (NRG 2025)
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying — the toolkit for studying AS: long-read, single-cell/spatial, CRISPR, sQTL, deep learning (NRG 2026)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — RNA G-quadruplexes as structural regulators of alternative splicing; cancer links & G4 ligands (Trends in Genetics 2026)

Concepts (16)
Machinery & chemistry

splicing — the process: introns/exons, two-step transesterification chemistry
spliceosome — the machine: five snRNPs, assembly cycle, RNA-based catalysis (ribozyme)
splice-sites — 5’ss, branch point, polypyrimidine tract, 3’ss; recognition & degenerate consensus

Regulation

splicing-regulatory-elements — exonic/intronic enhancers & silencers (ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS)
splicing-factors — SR proteins (activators) vs hnRNPs (repressors); tissue-specific factors
rna-binding-proteins — RBPs in splicing; motif reading; disease-linked prion-like domains
splicing-code — predicting isoforms from sequence; PWMs → deep learning (SpliceAI, Pangolin)
rna-structure-and-splicing — structural layer: RNA–RNA base pairing & G-quadruplexes mask/expose splice sites

Timing & coupling

cotranscriptional-splicing — splicing during transcription; coupling to Pol II elongation
post-transcriptional-splicing — splicing after transcription; intron retention; detained introns
splicing-and-chromatin — histone marks, adapters, Pol II speed, chromatin retention

Outcomes & significance

alternative-splicing — event types, prevalence, proteome expansion, functionality debate
splicing-and-disease — splice-site & splicing-factor mutations in disease and cancer
splicing-therapeutics — ASOs, small molecules, the first approved splicing drugs
splicing-and-evolution — AS scaling with complexity; drift vs selection; adaptations

Methods

splicing-methods — toolkit for studying AS: long-read, single-cell/spatial, CRISPR, sQTL, deep learning

Entities (8)
Splicing factors

sf3b1 — U2 snRNP/SF3b branch-point factor; mutated in MDS & uveal melanoma; drug target
u2af1 — 3’ splice-site recognition factor; recurrent myeloid-cancer mutations
srsf1 — prototypical SR protein (activator); proto-oncogene (ASF/SF2)
hnrnpa1 — prototypical hnRNP (repressor); SMN2/HIV; ALS prion-like domain
ptbp1 — polypyrimidine-tract repressor; PKM/Warburg switch (PTB/hnRNPI)

Genes / examples

dscam — extreme alternative splicing (tens of thousands of isoforms); neuronal self-avoidance
smn2 — spinal muscular atrophy gene; target of nusinersen & risdiplam

RNA structures

g-quadruplex — noncanonical G-rich fold regulating splicing; cancer/neuro links, G4 ligands

Tools

spliceai — landmark deep-learning splicing-code model (variant interpretation)

Syntheses

cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing — comparison of the two splicing timing modes (from a query)
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate — resolves the “AS functional vs noise” contradiction (Marasco vs Wright)

wiki/concepts/alternative-splicing.title: alternative-splicing
wiki/concepts/alternative-splicing.content:
Alternative Splicing
Alternative splicing (AS) is the process by which a single gene’s pre-mRNA is processed by the spliceosome into multiple distinct transcripts, expanding coding capacity and enabling quantitative regulation of protein levels wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. It is set up by cis-acting splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors and tissue-specific rna-binding-proteins, whose activator-vs-repressor effect is position-dependent — the logic the splicing-code aims to capture lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Event types. The canonical patterns are exon skipping (cassette exons), intron retention, mutually exclusive exons, and alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites. Their relative weight varies by lineage: exon skipping dominates in metazoans, intron retention in plants and fungi, partly reflecting intron- vs exon-definition modes of spliceosome assembly wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Prevalence and proteome expansion. AS occurs in ~90–95% of human genes, and RNA-seq estimates indicate >95–100% of human multiexon genes yield at least two isoforms marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternativelee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Extreme cases include Drosophila Dscam, generating tens of thousands of variants via mutually exclusive exons marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. AS scales with organismal complexity (splicing-and-evolution).
The functionality debate. Low-sensitivity mass spectrometry once suggested most alternative exons are irrelevant, but ribosome profiling shows ~75% of medium/high-abundance skipped-exon mRNAs are ribosome-engaged marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Marasco argues most AS is functional; Wright holds that most events are likely noise, with a conserved functional subset wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. Deep sequencing also detects low-abundance isoforms that may be spliceosomal errors lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This apparent clash is largely reconcilable — see alternative-splicing-functionality-debate (it turns on the definition of “functional”, the denominator counted, and a conserved head vs a noisy low-abundance tail).
AS–NMD and microexons. Coupled AS–NMD via “poison exons” introduces premature stop codons, autoregulating splicing-factors wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. Neural microexons (3–27 nt), promoted by SRRM4 and reduced in autism, are highly conserved regulators marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — prevalence, functionality (proteomics vs ribosome profiling), microexons
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — event types by lineage, noise-vs-function, AS–NMD poison exons
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — >95–100% multiexon prevalence, regulatory mechanism, error caveat
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — link to predictive modelling of AS outcomes

wiki/concepts/cotranscriptional-splicing.title: cotranscriptional-splicing
wiki/concepts/cotranscriptional-splicing.content:
Co-transcriptional Splicing
Co-transcriptional splicing is intron removal by the spliceosome that occurs during transcription, while the nascent pre-mRNA is still being synthesized by RNA polymerase II — the complement to post-transcriptional-splicing, which completes after transcription and polyadenylation choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. The spliceosome assembly steps (E → A → B → Bact/B* → C) are presumed identical in both modes; what differs is the timing relative to synthesis lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Most mammalian introns — roughly 70% — are excised on chromatin, much of it co-transcriptionally choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Kinetic coupling and the “window of opportunity”
Splice-site choice is set by cis-elements (splicing-regulatory-elements), trans splicing-factors, and coupling to Pol II elongation rate marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. In the kinetic coupling model, a slow polymerase widens the time window in which an upstream splice site can be recognized before a competing downstream one emerges: slow elongation promotes inclusion of class I exons and skipping of class II exons marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Pol II elongation runs at ~3–4 kb/min, and “slow” polymerase mutants alter splicing patterns lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This kinetics feeds directly into alternative-splicing outcomes, part of the broader splicing-code marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Elongation rate is itself modulated by chromatin: histone marks (e.g. H3K9ac, H3K9me2), nucleosomes and adapter proteins link splicing-and-chromatin to splicing outcomes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. The Pol II C-terminal domain (CTD) and co-transcriptional RNA folding further shape which rna-binding-proteins and factors engage the emerging transcript choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — co- vs post-transcriptional contrast; ~70% of introns excised on chromatin; identical assembly steps.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — kinetic coupling model; class I/II exons; elongation–chromatin link.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — Pol II ~3–4 kb/min; slow-polymerase effects; adapter proteins.

wiki/concepts/post-transcriptional-splic.title: post-transcriptional-splicing
wiki/concepts/post-transcriptional-splic.content:
Post-transcriptional Splicing
Post-transcriptional splicing is intron removal that completes after transcription and polyadenylation — typically while the polyadenylated transcript remains chromatin-associated — the complement to cotranscriptional-splicing choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. The spliceosome assembly steps are presumed identical to the co-transcriptional mode; only the timing differs choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Up to ~40% of mammalian introns are spliced post-transcriptionally (transcriptome-wide estimates of 27–45%), and >70% of expressed genes carry at least one post-transcriptionally retained intron choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Polyadenylated transcripts can linger on chromatin long after synthesis (median chromatin half-life ~50 min in K562, some genes >170 min), and splicing completion is a rate-limiting step for transcript release from chromatin, gating nuclear export and translation choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Retention, detained introns, and timing
Most retained introns are eventually excised productively rather than degraded; nuclear decay is the minority fate (~4% of protein-coding genes) choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Detained introns are a distinct regulated class — shorter, GC-rich, with weaker splice-sites — held until specific signals trigger removal, used in neurons, spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation and heat-shock responses choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Coupling to NMD provides another post-transcriptional control on protein output marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Timing is shaped by cis features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary structure) and trans splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins), with CLK/SRPK phosphorylation, PRMT methylation and m6A as modulators choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. Introns flanking alternative exons are preferentially spliced post-transcriptionally (67% vs 30% for constitutive), plausibly buying time for correct splice-site choice choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. What triggers the eventual reaction — and whether the delay precedes or follows spliceosome engagement — remains unknown choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. See splicing-and-chromatin for the retention machinery and splicing-and-disease (PRMT5/glioblastoma, FUS/ALS) for dysregulation.
Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — primary: ~40% post-transcriptional; detained introns; release gating; cis/trans determinants; unknown trigger.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — NMD-coupled post-transcriptional control of protein output.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — identical assembly steps; post-transcriptional splicing of slow/long introns.

wiki/concepts/rna-binding-proteins.title: rna-binding-proteins
wiki/concepts/rna-binding-proteins.content:
RNA-Binding Proteins
RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) are the broad class of proteins that read motifs in pre-mRNA and, among other functions, regulate splicing. In splicing they bind splicing-regulatory-elements to control how the spliceosome selects splice-sites and produces alternative-splicing rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. The splicing-dedicated RBPs — SR proteins, hnRNPs, RBM and CELF families, plus tissue-specific factors like Nova, PTB and Rbfox — are exactly the proteins covered as splicing-factors; this page treats RBPs more broadly, while splicing-factors focuses on the SR-activator vs hnRNP-repressor regulatory logic.
RBPs read short, often degenerate motifs, and because higher-eukaryote splice-sites resemble non-splice-site sequences, recognition depends on RBP/snRNP base pairing plus sequence context rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Their action is governed by the position-dependent splicing-code, with the Nova RNA map as a foundational example of motif-position mapping lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. RBP binding also intersects with RNA modifications: m6A readers (YTHDC1 recruiting SRSF3, hnRNPG/RBMX) modulate exon inclusion rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
RBPs are central to splicing-and-disease: hnRNPA1, TDP-43 and FUS carry prion-like domains implicated in ALS lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, and many tissue-specific RBPs (e.g. RBM20/titin in cardiomyopathy) drive homeostasis and disease marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. The same disease-relevant factors recur as splicing-factor mutations in cancer rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — RBPs as position-dependent regulators (Nova map); hnRNPA1/TDP-43/FUS prion-like domains in ALS
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — RBP families reading context-dependent motifs; m6A readers; disease/cancer mutations
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — tissue-specific RBPs (RBM20/titin) in physiology and disease

wiki/concepts/rna-structure-and-splicing.title: rna-structure-and-splicing
wiki/concepts/rna-structure-and-splicing.content:
RNA Structure and Splicing
Beyond linear sequence motifs, the secondary and higher-order structure of
pre-mRNA helps determine splicing outcomes. Where splice-sites and
splicing-regulatory-elements describe splicing control as a sequence code,
this page collects the structural layer — folded elements that mask or expose
splice sites and tune where splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins
bind. Structure is an explicit part of what the splicing-code must capture
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
RNA–RNA base pairing
Local and long-range base pairing can dictate splice-site choice. The classic
example is Drosophila dscam, where conserved “docking” and “selector”
sequences pair to select one of 48 mutually exclusive exons; in vivo chemical
probing shows the transcriptome can be highly structured
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. RNA secondary structure is
also one of the cis determinants that shape splicing timing
(post-transcriptional-splicing) alongside GC content, intron length and
splice-site strength choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
G-quadruplexes (rG4s)
A prominent structural regulator is the g-quadruplex — a noncanonical
four-stranded fold in guanine-rich RNA. rG4s near 5′/3′ splice sites act
position-dependently as enhancers or silencers across all alternative-splicing
event types, by recruiting or blocking factors and by masking/exposing splice
sites; ~60% of transcripts contain at least one putative quadruplex sequence and
splice junctions are ~3-fold enriched for G4s on the coding strand
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes. G4-reader proteins include the
hnRNP family, FMRP and SRSF1; DNA G4s can also act indirectly via Pol II
elongation and R-loops (cotranscriptional-splicing)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Disease and therapeutic angle
Structural mis-regulation is tied to cancer and neurological disease (e.g. TP53,
BCL-x, hTERT; FMR1/Fragile X), and G4-stabilising ligands are an emerging way to
modulate splicing — a structural complement to sequence-targeted ASOs
(splicing-and-disease, splicing-therapeutics)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Sources

peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — primary: rG4s as structural AS regulators, readers, disease, ligands
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — RNA–RNA base pairing (DSCAM); structured transcriptome
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — structure as part of the splicing code
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — RNA structure as a cis determinant of splicing timing

wiki/concepts/splice-sites.title: splice-sites
wiki/concepts/splice-sites.content:
Splice sites
Splice sites are the conserved sequence motifs at exon–intron boundaries that the spliceosome recognizes to define what is removed (intron) versus retained (exon) during splicing editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. Each intron is delimited by four core signals: the 5’ splice site (at the exon–intron junction), the branch point (BP), the polypyrimidine tract (PPT), and the 3’ splice site rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Recognition
These signals are recognized through base pairing of snRNPs and splicing-factors: U1 snRNA base-pairs with the 5’ splice site, and its complementarity defines 5’ss “strength”; the 3’ end of the intron is recognized by SF1 (branch point), U2AF2/U2AF1 (PPT and 3’ss), and the U2 snRNP (including SF3B1/PHF5A) at the branch point rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Some positions are near-invariant: a G at the +1 position of the 5’ss is required for catalysis, and a pyrimidine at +2 is present in 99.6% of 5’ splice sites rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Degeneracy enables alternative splicing
In higher eukaryotes splice sites are remarkably diverse and often resemble non-splice-site sequences, so reliable recognition depends on sequence context plus auxiliary splicing-regulatory-elements rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. This weak, degenerate metazoan consensus is what makes splice-site choice tunable, enabling alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Internal exons are constrained to ~50–250 nt by exon definition rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Mutations that disrupt splice sites can cause exon skipping, intron retention, or activation of cryptic splice sites, contributing to splicing-and-disease editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — BP/PPT/3’ss recognition by SF1, U2AF, U2; U1 base-pairing and 5’ss strength; +1 G, +2 pyrimidine; degeneracy
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — weak metazoan splice sites underpinning alternative splicing
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — conserved boundary motifs; variant effects (skipping, retention, cryptic sites)

wiki/concepts/spliceosome.title: spliceosome
wiki/concepts/spliceosome.content:
Spliceosome
The spliceosome is the dynamic ribonucleoprotein (RNP) machine that carries out splicing, removing introns via a two-step transesterification reaction editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. In humans it is roughly ~4.8 MDa and is built from five small nuclear RNP particles (snRNPs) — U1, U2, U4/U6 and U5 — together with numerous proteins: more than 200 in metazoans (more than 150 additional proteins beyond the snRNAs) versus ~100 in yeast lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Assembly cycle
The spliceosome is not preassembled; it assembles stepwise on every intron and then recycles, passing through ordered states E → A → B → Bact/B* → C lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Its composition is highly dynamic: the B → Bact transition loses the U1 and U4 snRNAs and ~35 proteins while adding 12, and Bact → C loses 2 proteins and adds 9 lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Cryo-EM has now resolved many of these conformational states rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
RNA-based catalysis
The spliceosome is a ribozyme: its catalytic center is RNA — the base-paired U2–U6 snRNA complex — and metal-ion-rescue experiments in yeast U6 snRNA show that RNA, not the prp8 protein, coordinates the catalytic divalent metals lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This makes it structurally and mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns, supporting an evolutionary origin from them (splicing-and-evolution) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Key proteins
prp8 sits at the active-site core and contains RNase H- and reverse-transcriptase-like domains; SF3b (including SF3B1) participates in branch-point recognition by U2 and is a drug target lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. The spliceosome works with splice-sites and splicing-factors.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — ~4.8 MDa size, snRNP composition, E→C assembly cycle, ribozyme/group II analogy, prp8
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — five snRNPs + >150 proteins; cryo-EM snapshots; SF3B1
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — dynamic RNP, two-step transesterification, resolved conformational states

wiki/concepts/splicing-and-chromatin.title: splicing-and-chromatin
wiki/concepts/splicing-and-chromatin.content:
Splicing and Chromatin
Chromatin state is coupled to splicing through histone marks, adapter proteins, nucleosome positioning and RNA polymerase II elongation speed, which together influence cotranscriptional-splicing outcomes and the retention of pre-mRNA on chromatin lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Histone marks, adapters, and nucleosomes
Histone modifications recruit splicing machinery and tune elongation. “Adapter” proteins physically bridge chromatin to the spliceosome and splicing-factors: CHD1 links chromatin to the U2 snRNP, and MRG15 (an H3K36me3 reader) recruits the repressor PTB to regulate alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Marks such as H3K9ac and H3K9me2 modulate Pol II elongation and thus splicing outcomes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. A documented feedback loop: G9a (EHMT2) exon-10 inclusion creates an H3K9me2-driven positive loop reinforcing neuronal differentiation marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Pol II speed and chromatin retention
Pol II elongation (~3–4 kb/min) sets the kinetic “window” for splice-site recognition; nucleosomes act as elongation barriers, and slow-polymerase variants shift splicing patterns lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Beyond synthesis, polyadenylated pre-mRNA can remain chromatin-associated long after transcription ends, where splicing completion gates release — making chromatin retention central to post-transcriptional-splicing choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Nuclear compartmentalization
Subnuclear localization correlates with splicing timing: ~31% of nuclear-speckle-associated retained introns overlap annotated detained introns, versus 12% for lamina-associated ones — implicating nuclear speckles and other compartments in coupling transcript position to splicing completion choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. How speckles mechanistically link localization to timing remains an open question choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — adapter proteins (CHD1–U2, MRG15–PTB); Pol II speed; histone-mark coupling.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — H3K9ac/H3K9me2 and elongation; G9a/H3K9me2 neuronal feedback loop.
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — chromatin retention of polyadenylated pre-mRNA; nuclear speckles vs lamina.

wiki/concepts/splicing-and-disease.title: splicing-and-disease
wiki/concepts/splicing-and-disease.content:
Splicing and Disease
Errors in splicing are a major source of human pathology, arising both from mutations in the pre-mRNA itself and from mutations in the splicing-factors that interpret it.
Cis-acting mutations
Mutations in consensus splice-sites account for roughly 10% of human inherited disease wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. Mutations in splicing-regulatory-elements (enhancers/silencers) also cause disease — examples include β-globin thalassemia lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Because the splicing-code is context-dependent, many disease variants act by disrupting exon definition: ~90% of BRCA2 5′ss mutations in breast cancer affect splicing rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Single-gene disorders

Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA): loss of SMN1; a C>T difference makes the paralog SMN2 skip exon 7, yielding insufficient functional protein marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD): dystrophin exon deletions disrupt the reading frame marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Familial dysautonomia: an IKBKAP intron-20 +6 mutation causes exon skipping rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.

Cancer
Recurrent somatic mutations in core splicing-factors drive myeloid malignancies and others: SF3B1, U2AF1, SRSF2 and ZRSR2 in myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and CLL lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. SF3B1 mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts, 15–36% of eye/uveal melanomas, and are among the most common single mutations in CLL; mutant SF3B1 activates cryptic 3′ss and represses the tumour suppressor BRD9 via a poison exon rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. SRSF1 acts as a proto-oncogene marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Neurodegeneration
Prion-like-domain mutations in rna-binding-proteins hnRNPA1, TDP-43 and FUS cause ALS lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. These disorders motivate splicing-therapeutics.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — splicing-factor mutations in cancer; BRCA2; familial dysautonomia.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — SMA, DMD, oncogenic SRSF1.
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — ~10% of inherited disease from splice-site mutations.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — thalassemia, ALS, myeloid splicing-factor mutations.

wiki/concepts/splicing-and-evolution.title: splicing-and-evolution
wiki/concepts/splicing-and-evolution.content:
Splicing and Evolution
alternative-splicing (AS) is an evolutionarily labile process that can fuel adaptation, speciation and organismal complexity wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Scaling with complexity
AS prevalence correlates with organismal complexity: pre-RNA-seq Ensembl estimates rise from C. elegans (25%) and Drosophila (45%) to mouse (63%) and human (88%) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. AS is “more a rule than an exception” in multicellular eukaryotes and prevails in K-selected lineages, offering a readily available pool of mRNA variability co-opted without making new genes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Drift versus selection; cis versus trans
Most AS events are probably noise and non-conserved, but a functional subset diversifies the proteome wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. AS profiles diverge faster than gene expression — vertebrate organ transcriptomes track species identity more than organ type marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Novel splice patterns arise via cis-effects (splice-sites/splicing-regulatory-elements changes, exonization, intronization) and trans-effects (mutations in splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins); cis effects appear more common in interspecies divergence wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Concrete adaptations

Stickleback MSX2A: an exon-1 mutation creates an ISE that activates an alternative 5′ss → truncated protein → shorter spines in freshwater fish wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Vampire bat TRPV1: trigeminal-specific exon-14a inclusion yields a short isoform sensing ~30 °C for infrared prey detection wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Cavefish (Astyanax) per2 exon-21 skipping → truncated Per2 enhancing lipogenesis, convergent across species wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.

Origin of the machinery
The spliceosome is a ribozyme whose RNA catalytic core is mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns, from which it is thought to have evolved lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. AS also drives splicing-and-disease and links to splicing-therapeutics.
Sources

wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — drift vs selection, cis/trans divergence, stickleback/bat/cavefish adaptations.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — adaptive value of AS, species-tracking transcriptomes.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — AS scaling with complexity; group II intron origin of the spliceosome.
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — joint evolution of splicing and protein codes.

wiki/concepts/splicing-code.title: splicing-code
wiki/concepts/splicing-code.content:
Splicing Code
The splicing code is the idea that splicing outcomes are written in genomic sequence and can be predicted from it. The “ideal” model would predict the full set of transcript isoforms and their frequencies from sequence alone in any cellular context, while approximating the underlying biology read by the spliceosome and splicing-factors capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. The code combines four core motifs at splice-sites with position-dependent splicing-regulatory-elements interpreted by rna-binding-proteins capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-thelee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Model lineage. Capitanchik traces ~30 years of evolution capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the:

Core-motif models scoring splice-sites (PWMs, MaxEntScan).
Integrative feature models using ~1,000+ predefined features (Splicing Code, SPANR).
End-to-end deep learning that learns features de novo (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice). SpliceAI is a 32-layer residual CNN; Pangolin shares the architecture but trains across species and tissues, adding tissue-specific splice-site strength.
Whole-locus / omics models (Borzoi, BigRNA) taking up to ~0.5 Mb input and predicting tissue-specific RNA-seq plus other functional genomics.
Self-supervised sequence LLMs (SpliceBERT, Nucleotide Transformer).

Context window matters: extending SpliceAI’s input from 80→400 nt raised top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95% capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
Clinical variant interpretation. SpliceAI and Pangolin predict non-coding cryptic-splicing variants — roughly 10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations — informing ClinGen/ACMG classification, consistent with the long-known ~10% of inherited disease caused by consensus-site mutations capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-thewright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. This grounds splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics applications.
Open problems. Black-box accuracy outpaces mechanistic interpretability; tissue-specific prediction is weak (median Spearman ~0.22–0.43), limited by scarce training data; and predicting full-length isoforms — not just per-site usage — remains unsolved, awaiting long-read and single-cell data capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
Sources

capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — full model lineage, accuracy data, clinical use, open problems
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — position-dependent regulatory-element basis of the code
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — ~10% of inherited disease from splice-site mutations

wiki/concepts/splicing-factors.title: splicing-factors
wiki/concepts/splicing-factors.content:
Splicing Factors
Splicing factors are the trans-acting proteins that bind splicing-regulatory-elements and steer the spliceosome’s choice of splice-sites, thereby setting up alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. They are a subset of rna-binding-proteins dedicated to splicing; this page focuses on their splicing roles, while broader RBP biology is on the rna-binding-proteins page.
The two classic families are antagonistic: SR proteins generally act as activators that promote exon inclusion, whereas hnRNPs generally act as repressors lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This activator/repressor split is not absolute — the effect of a given factor is position-dependent relative to the regulated exon, the basis of the splicing-code (e.g. the Nova RNA map) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Many splicing factors are tissue-specific: Nova, PTB (PTBP1), Rbfox, and Muscleblind (MBNL)/CELF tune splicing in particular cell types lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. SRRM4 coordinates neural microexon recognition and is reduced in autism rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative; MBNL1/MBNL2 control the FOXP1 differentiation/pluripotency switch, and hnRNPA1/A2 and PTBP1 govern the PKM Warburg-effect switch marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Activity is also regulated post-translationally and via cross/auto-regulation: CLK1 phosphorylates SR proteins as a body-temperature sensor marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and mutations in core factors (SF3B1, U2AF1/2) drive splicing-and-disease, notably myeloid cancers rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SR-vs-hnRNP activator/repressor split, position-dependence, tissue-specific Nova/PTB/Rbfox/MBNL/CELF
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — splicing code, SRRM4 microexons, core-factor disease mutations
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — CLK1/SR phosphorylation, MBNL/FOXP1 and hnRNP/PTBP1 gene-specific switches

wiki/concepts/splicing-methods.title: splicing-methods
wiki/concepts/splicing-methods.content:
Splicing Methods
The experimental and computational toolkit for studying alternative-splicing
— how isoforms are mapped, how their function is tested, and how their genetic
control is decoded. For decades technical barriers limited the field; recent
advances (long-read sequencing, CRISPR functional assays, population genetics,
deep learning) are now transforming it
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Mapping isoforms (sequencing)

Short-read RNA-seq quantifies splicing either isoform-based or event-based
(exon/junction), summarised as PSI / ΔPSI (percent-spliced-in); different
tools (e.g. rMATS-style event callers, MAJIQ, LeafCutter) can disagree, so
orthogonal validation is needed sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Long-read sequencing (PacBio HiFi ~99.8% / 10–25 kb; Oxford Nanopore cDNA
10–20 kb) resolves full-length exon connectivity; ONT direct RNA-seq reads
native RNA and modifications (m6A) sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Single-cell full-length (Smart-seq2/3, VASA-seq) and long-read single-cell
(ScISOr-Seq2, MAS-ISO-seq, snISOr-Seq) and spatial methods (MERFISH,
Slide-seq, Stereo-seq, Spl-ISO-Seq) extend isoform views to cell-type and
tissue resolution sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.

Testing function

CRISPR is the new functional layer: Cas9 editing/fitness screens, base
editors targeting splice-sites, and RNA-targeting Cas13/dCasRx for
isoform-specific perturbation sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Minigene / reporter and MPRA assays (millions of synthetic sequences)
dissect splicing-regulatory-elements and variant effects
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.

Decoding genetic control

Population genetics — splicing QTLs (sQTL), allele-specific expression,
and atlases (GTEx, ENCODE) map how variation shapes splicing and
disease risk; MPRAs found ~10% of exonic disease
mutations disrupt splicing vs ~3% of common SNPs
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Deep-learning splicing-code models (spliceai, Pangolin, DeltaSplice,
Borzoi, AlphaGenome) predict outcomes from sequence but do not yet capture
cell-type-specific isoform repertoires
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying,
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Why it matters here
These tools are exactly what the alternative-splicing-functionality-debate
identified as the way to settle whether isoforms are functional or noise —
long-read sequencing, single-cell, and CRISPR perturbation make isoforms
directly measurable and testable sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Sources

sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying — primary: the full
experimental + computational toolkit, with metrics and caveats
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — the deep-learning
splicing-code modelling lineage

wiki/concepts/splicing-regulatory-elemen.title: splicing-regulatory-elements
wiki/concepts/splicing-regulatory-elemen.content:
Splicing Regulatory Elements
Splicing regulatory elements are short cis-acting RNA sequences in pre-mRNA that tune how the spliceosome recognizes splice-sites and so shape alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. They are classified by location and effect into four types: exonic splicing enhancers (ESEs), exonic splicing silencers (ESSs), intronic splicing enhancers (ISEs), and intronic splicing silencers (ISSs) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
These elements work by recruiting trans-acting splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins: enhancers typically bind SR proteins that promote exon definition and splice-site usage, while silencers typically bind hnRNPs (and related proteins such as RBM and CELF) that repress recognition rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Crucially, the effect of an element is position-dependent: the same motif can act positively or negatively depending on where it sits relative to a splice site, a principle central to the splicing-code rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Splice-site choice thus depends jointly on cis-elements, trans factors, and coupling to transcription (cotranscriptional-splicing) marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Because they govern exon inclusion, these elements are common disease targets: two-thirds of all possible mutations in an alternative exon affect inclusion rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and mutations in silencers/enhancers cause disease lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. They are also therapeutic handles (splicing-therapeutics): nusinersen blocks an intron-7 ISS to boost SMN2 exon 7 inclusion, and eteplirsen targets an ESE to skip a dystrophin exon marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — defines exonic/intronic enhancers and silencers and their position-dependent, protein-recruiting role
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS classification, recruited RBP families, splicing code, mutation/therapeutic relevance
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — joint cis/trans/transcription control; ISS- and ESE-targeting drugs

wiki/concepts/splicing-therapeutics.title: splicing-therapeutics
wiki/concepts/splicing-therapeutics.content:
Splicing Therapeutics
Understanding the splicing-code has made splicing a druggable process, turning splicing-and-disease into a target for several now-clinical interventions.
Antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs)
ASOs base-pair with the pre-mRNA to redirect splice-sites selection. Nusinersen (FDA-approved 2016) blocks an intronic splicing silencer bound by hnRNPA1/A2 to boost SMN2 exon-7 inclusion in SMA, delivered by intrathecal injection rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Eteplirsen targets an exonic splicing enhancer to skip a dystrophin exon in DMD, restoring an in-frame, internally truncated (Becker-like) protein rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Small molecules
Risdiplam (FDA-approved 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone, “repairs” the U1–SMN2 exon-7 interaction by aiding U1C zinc-finger contact rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. In oncology, SF3B1 inhibitors (pladienolide B, H3B-8800 in trials, spliceostatin A, sudemycins) block the open→closed branch-point-recognition switch of the spliceosome rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Indisulam degrades the splicing-factors component RBM39, exploiting synthetic lethality rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Immunotherapy and engineered machinery
Splicing modulators generate tumour neoantigens, combinable with anti-PD1 checkpoint blockade rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Engineered U1 snRNAs can be redesigned to recognize mutant splice sites (proposed for familial dysautonomia) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing is an emerging route to correct splice defects directly lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Role of the splicing code
Rational design depends on predicting how a sequence change alters splicing — splicing-regulatory-elements recognized by rna-binding-proteins — and on computational splicing-code models (SpliceAI, MMSplice, Pangolin) trained by saturation mutagenesis rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — risdiplam, SF3B1/RBM39 drugs, neoantigens, U1 snRNAs, splicing-code models.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — nusinersen and eteplirsen mechanisms.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — spliceostatin/SF3b, ASOs, CRISPR.

wiki/concepts/splicing.title: splicing
wiki/concepts/splicing.content:
Splicing
Splicing is the process that removes introns from a pre-mRNA and ligates the flanking exons together to produce mature mRNA, an essential step for accurate translation of genetic information editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. Most eukaryotic genes are organized as a “mosaic” of introns (non-coding, removed) and exons (retained), a “split gene” architecture discovered in 1977 by Richard Roberts and Phillip Sharp and recognized with the 1993 Nobel Prize editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. The boundaries between introns and exons are defined by conserved splice-sites.
Chemistry
Splicing is carried out by the spliceosome through a two-step transesterification reaction editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. In the first step, the branch-point adenosine attacks the 5’ splice site; in the second step, the freed 5’ exon attacks the 3’ splice site, joining the two exons and releasing the intron. Because the same boundaries are recognized multiple times during assembly, this provides a built-in principle of accuracy rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Significance
The split-gene organization makes alternative-splicing possible: by regulating which exons are included or excluded, a single gene can yield multiple mRNA isoforms, greatly expanding coding potential and affecting mRNA stability, localization and translation editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. More than 95% of human multi-intron genes are alternatively spliced editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Splicing can occur cotranscriptional-splicing or post-transcriptional-splicing, and disruptions to it underlie splicing-and-disease.
Sources

editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — split-gene mosaic, two-step chemistry, mature mRNA, historical discovery
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — intron removal by the spliceosome; alternative splicing prevalence
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — two-step transesterification; multiple-recognition accuracy principle

wiki/entities/dscam.title: dscam
wiki/entities/dscam.content:
Dscam
What: The Drosophila Down syndrome cell adhesion molecule gene, the canonical extreme exemplar of alternative-splicing, whose mutually exclusive exon clusters can combinatorially generate tens of thousands of isoforms.
Dscam arranges alternative exons into four clusters used in a mutually exclusive manner. lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative reports this can combinatorially generate >36,000 distinct spliced mRNA isoforms — roughly three times the fly’s gene count — with the exon-6 cluster alone containing 48 mutually exclusive exons. marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative states the gene can give rise to up to 38,000 mRNA variants, half (~19,000) encoding distinct extracellular domains. Using long-read RNA sequencing, wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source identified 7,874 complete transcript isoforms of Dscam1, thousands essential for normal neural circuit development.
Selection within a cluster is dictated by RNA–RNA base pairing: a conserved “docking” site base-pairs with a complementary “selector” site upstream of each exon-6 variant, ensuring exactly one is included (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative). The hnRNP protein hrp36 enforces mutual exclusivity by repressing the other exons. This makes Dscam the best example of cis-acting RNA secondary structure controlling splice-sites choice, a mechanism beyond protein splicing-factors alone.
Why it matters
Each neuron stochastically expresses a unique subset of DSCAM isoforms; identical extracellular domains bind homophilically and trigger repulsion, enabling neuronal self-avoidance (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). It is the showcase for how alternative-splicing expands proteomic and functional diversity far beyond gene number (splicing-and-evolution).
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — >36,000 isoforms, 48 exon-6 exons, docking/selector base pairing, hrp36 repressor.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — up to 38,000 variants (~19,000 extracellular domains); self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion.
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — long-read RNA-seq found 7,874 complete Dscam1 isoforms.

wiki/entities/g-quadruplex.title: g-quadruplex
wiki/entities/g-quadruplex.content:
G-quadruplex
What: A noncanonical four-stranded nucleic-acid secondary structure formed in
guanine-rich regions, where four guanines Hoogsteen-bond into a G-quartet and
stacked quartets are stabilised by K⁺; it forms in both DNA (dG4) and RNA (rG4)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Role in splicing
rG4s are an emerging structural layer of alternative-splicing regulation
(rna-structure-and-splicing). Positioned near 5′/3′ splice-sites, they act
position-dependently as enhancers or silencers across all event types — by
recruiting or blocking splicing-factors and by remodelling the pre-mRNA to
mask or expose splice sites. dG4s additionally influence splicing indirectly
via RNA Pol II elongation and R-loops (cotranscriptional-splicing)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Reader proteins named in the source include the hnRNP family (F, H1/H, L),
FMRP (RGG motif), the AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Significance

Prevalence: ~60% of transcripts carry ≥1 putative quadruplex sequence (~1.2M
PQSs); splice junctions are ~3-fold enriched for G4s on the coding strand.
Disease: linked to cancer (TP53, BCL-x, hTERT, EWS-FLI)
and neurological disease (FMR1/Fragile X, BACE1/Alzheimer).
Therapeutics: G4-stabilising ligands (e.g.
pyridostatin, PhenDC3) can modulate splicing, though effects are sometimes
contradictory and ligand selectivity remains unsolved.
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes

Sources

peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — definition, mechanism, readers, prevalence, disease, ligands

wiki/entities/hnrnpa1.title: hnrnpa1
wiki/entities/hnrnpa1.content:
hnRNPA1
What: The prototypical hnRNP, a well-studied splicing factor that represses splicing by binding silencer elements.
Human hnRNPA1 is the archetypal repressor among the hnRNP family of rna-binding-proteins, the silencing arm of the splicing-code. It binds exonic and intronic splicing silencers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) to fine-tune alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This contrasts with SR activators like srsf1; consistent with the position-dependent rule, hnRNPA1 can in some cases act as an activator, and can even promote U1 snRNP binding during splicing silencing.
Role in splicing
hnRNPA1 has several mechanisms: (a) binding a silencer to block recognition of nearby splice-sites; (b) binding a high-affinity site, then cooperatively spreading to adjacent low-affinity sites; and (c) looping of pre-mRNA via interactions between hnRNPA1 proteins bound at flanking intronic sites, promoting exon exclusion lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. It regulates smn2 and HIV pre-mRNA splicing, and (with hnRNPA2/PTBP1) drives the PKM Warburg switch marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Disease / significance
Cis-acting mutations in the prion-like, glycine-rich C-terminal domain of hnRNPA1 cause amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Such prion-like low-complexity domains are shared with hnRNPA2B1, TDP-43, FUS, EWSR1 and TAF15; TDP-43 and FUS prion-like-domain mutations are likewise linked to ALS (splicing-and-disease) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — repressor mechanisms (blocking, spreading, looping), SMN2/HIV, ALS prion-like domain
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — PKM/Warburg switch; SMN2 intron-7 ISS

wiki/entities/ptbp1.title: ptbp1
wiki/entities/ptbp1.content:
PTBP1
What: The polypyrimidine-tract binding protein, an hnRNP-class splicing factor and tissue-specific repressor of splicing.
PTBP1 (PTB/hnRNPI) is a tissue-specific RNA-binding protein that, like other hnRNPs, mainly represses alternative-splicing by binding pyrimidine-rich splicing-regulatory-elements. As with hnrnpa1, its activator-versus-repressor effect is position-dependent, the hallmark of the splicing-code that distinguishes it from SR activators such as srsf1 lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
PTBP1 (with hnRNPA1 and hnRNPA2) controls the PKM E9/E10 mutually exclusive exons, raising the PKM2:PKM1 ratio to enable the Warburg effect in cancer; the genes encoding these regulators are activated by MYC marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. PTBP1 also links splicing to splicing-and-chromatin: the adapter MRG15 binds both PTB and the H3K36me3 histone mark, targeting PTB to transcripts such as FGFR2 exon IIIb lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Disease / significance
PTBP1 is a determinant of splicing timing: it is among the proteins associated with post-transcriptional-splicing, where RNA loops mediated by PTBP1 shape regulation after transcription, complementing cotranscriptional-splicing rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Across species, a PTBP1 isoform reshapes splicing, tying it to organismal/evolutionary differences marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Its PKM control connects it to cancer metabolism (splicing-and-disease).
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — repressor; MRG15/H3K36me3 chromatin adapter; FGFR2
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — PKM/Warburg switch; cross-species isoform
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — post-transcriptional splicing timing; PTBP1 RNA loops

wiki/entities/sf3b1.title: sf3b1
wiki/entities/sf3b1.content:
SF3B1
What: A core U2 snRNP / SF3b component that recognizes the intron branch point during 3’-end definition by the spliceosome.
SF3B1 is one of the splicing-factors that, together with SF1, the U2AF heterodimer (u2af1/U2AF2) and U2 snRNP, define the 3’ end of introns at splice-sites rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
SF3B1 and PHF5A have a key role in branch-point (BP) recognition. A major rearrangement of SF3B1 from an open to a closed conformation is triggered by recognition of the pre-mRNA, with its HEAT-repeat domain contacting the BP adenosine, which is sandwiched between SF3B1 and PHF5A — a fidelity checkpoint confirming proper BP recognition rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Disease & therapeutics
SF3B1 (also called SAP155) is recurrently mutated in myeloid malignancies and other cancers lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts and 15–36% of eye melanomas; K700E correlates with worse CLL prognosis but better MDS prognosis. Mutant SF3B1 activates cryptic 3’ss 10–30 nt upstream and converges on repressing the tumour suppressor BRD9 via a poison exon (splicing-and-disease). Small molecules (H3B-8800 in trials, pladienolide B, spliceostatin A) block the open→closed change (splicing-therapeutics) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — BP recognition mechanism, mutation spectrum, inhibitors
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SF3b/SAP155 in myeloid cancer; spliceostatin
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — convergence of myeloid splicing-factor mutations

wiki/entities/smn2.title: smn2
wiki/entities/smn2.content:
SMN2
What: The human survival motor neuron 2 gene, a near-duplicate of SMN1 that fails to produce enough functional protein because of an alternative-splicing defect — the central target in spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) therapy.
Spinal muscular atrophy is caused by loss of SMN1; SMN2 persists but cannot compensate (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). The two genes are ~30 kbp duplicates differing in only 11 nucleotides, the key change being a C>T substitution in SMN2 exon 7 that increases its skipping (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). The predominant SMN2 mRNA therefore lacks exon 7 (E7) and yields a truncated, inactive SMN protein, while the minor E7-included variant is full-length and functional.
Both pre-mRNAs carry a splicing silencer in intron 7 bound by the repressive splicing-factors hnrnpa1 and hnRNPA2, which promote E7 skipping (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). This is a textbook case of splicing-and-disease driven by silencer-controlled exon definition (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).
Why it matters
SMN2 is the proof-of-concept target for splicing-therapeutics. Nusinersen (approved 2016), an antisense oligonucleotide, blocks the intron-7 silencer to displace hnrnpa1/hnRNPA2 and boost E7 inclusion (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). Risdiplam (FDA 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone small molecule, has similar effects by stabilizing U1 snRNA base-pairing at the SMN2 E7 5′ splice site (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). These were among the first splicing-corrective drugs.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — 11-nt SMN1/SMN2 difference, C>T in E7, intron-7 hnRNPA1/A2 silencer, nusinersen/risdiplam.
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — SMA from SMN1 loss; nusinersen ISS-blocking ASO; risdiplam mechanism on U1–E7 pairing.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SMN2/SMA as a silencer-driven splicing disease and antisense target.

wiki/entities/spliceai.title: spliceai
wiki/entities/spliceai.content:
SpliceAI
What: A landmark end-to-end deep-learning model of the splicing-code that predicts splice-sites directly from genomic sequence.
SpliceAI is a 32-layer deep residual convolutional neural network trained on human GENCODE sequence, learning features de novo rather than from predefined motifs capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. It marks a shift in the model lineage from core-motif scorers (PWMs, MaxEntScan) and feature-engineered integrative models toward learned representations, and it sits alongside related deep models — Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice — and feeds into large omics-prediction models (Borzoi, BigRNA) and self-supervised sequence language models.
How it works
The model is tasked to call donor and acceptor sites from raw sequence. Context window size is decisive: extending input from 80 nt to 400 nt raised top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95%, and going to 10,000 nt added a further 3–5% capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. Accuracy on lncRNAs (lacking coding bias) was similar to mRNAs (top-k ~84%). In silico mutagenesis showed it indirectly predicts nucleosome positions, contributing to exon-boundary determination (splicing-and-chromatin).
Significance & limits
SpliceAI predicts non-coding cryptic-splicing variants — estimated ~10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations — and underpins clinical splicing-and-disease workflows: it reached AUC 0.959 on BRCA1 near-splice-site variants and informs ClinGen/ACMG variant classification. Limits remain: exonic and distal variants are predicted poorly, tissue-specific prediction is weak, and black-box interpretability is an open problem.
Sources

capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — describes SpliceAI’s architecture, context-window effect, clinical use, and place in the splicing-code model lineage.

wiki/entities/srsf1.title: srsf1
wiki/entities/srsf1.content:
SRSF1
What: The prototypical SR protein, a sequence-specific splicing factor that activates splicing and behaves as a proto-oncogene when overexpressed.
SRSF1 is a member of the SR-protein family of rna-binding-proteins that constitute the activating arm of the splicing-code. It typically binds exonic splicing enhancers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) and promotes inclusion of nearby exons during alternative-splicing. This is the classic counterpoint to hnRNP repressors like hnrnpa1: SR activators and hnRNP silencers act in a position-dependent manner, so the same factor or motif can promote or suppress an exon depending on where it binds lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
SRSF1 (ASF/SF2) can promote use of a proximal 5’ splice site (see splice-sites) by increasing U1 snRNP–pre-mRNA binding lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. An SF2/ASF-dependent exonic splicing enhancer in smn2 exon 7 illustrates how its loss alters disease-relevant splicing.
Disease / significance
SRSF1 overexpression acts as an oncogene in mice and qualifies the SRSF1 gene as a proto-oncogene in many human tumours, with targets in the mTORC1 pathway lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Its oncogenic function depends on methylation by PRMT5; SRSF1 also shuttles between nucleus and cytoplasm, making PRMT5 inhibitors candidate therapeutics (splicing-and-disease, splicing-therapeutics) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SR activator mechanism, proximal 5’ss/U1, proto-oncogene
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — proto-oncogene; PRMT5 methylation
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — PRMT5 dependence, nucleocytoplasmic shuttling

wiki/entities/u2af1.title: u2af1
wiki/entities/u2af1.content:
U2AF1
What: The small subunit of the U2AF heterodimer that recognizes the 3’ splice-site AG dinucleotide during intron 3’-end definition.
U2AF1 (also called U2AF35) is one of the splicing-factors that define the 3’ end of introns at splice-sites, acting alongside SF1, sf3b1 and U2 snRNP rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
The U2AF heterodimer (U2AF1–U2AF2/U2AF65) binds three adjacent 3’-end sequence motifs: U2AF1 contacts the 3’ss AG dinucleotide while U2AF2 binds the polypyrimidine tract rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. This makes U2AF1 a determinant of 3’ss selection. As a RNA-binding step in 3’-end recognition, its binding can be modulated — e.g. m6A at the 3’ss AG inhibits U2AF1 binding, leading to intron retention rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Disease & therapeutics
U2AF1 (U2AF35) is recurrently somatically mutated in myeloid cancers including myelodysplastic syndrome lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Mutations alter which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected: S34F/Y mutants favour inclusion of exons with CAG 3’ss and disfavour UAG, whereas Q157P/R mutants favour AG/A 3’ss rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing (splicing-and-disease). U2AF1, SRSF2 and sf3b1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — 3’ss recognition, S34/Q157 mutant effects, m6A
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — U2AF35 recurrent mutations in MDS/CLL
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — convergence of myeloid splicing-factor mutations

wiki/overview.title: overview
wiki/overview.content:
Overview
The evolving top-level thesis of this wiki. Currently the wiki covers a single
topic — splicing — built from 9 review-level sources (2015–2026). Grounded
strictly in the locally ingested sources.
Current thesis (topic: splicing)
RNA splicing is a central layer of gene regulation, and across these sources
the field’s center of gravity has shifted from how splicing works to how it
is controlled and read:

The machinery is essentially solved. The spliceosome is a large,
dynamic, RNA-catalysed machine (a ribozyme related to group II introns), and
cryo-EM has resolved most of its states
(lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative,
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). Remaining questions are
about regulation, not chemistry.
Outcomes are set by a position-dependent code. Weak metazoan
splice-sites + splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors
determine isoform choice. Modeling this splicing-code has progressed from
position weight matrices to deep-learning models that now interpret ~10% of
pathogenic rare-disease variants
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).
Timing is itself regulatory. Splicing is largely co-transcriptional, but
a large fraction (~40%) is post-transcriptional-splicing, gating mRNA
export and translation (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Function vs noise is unresolved. alternative-splicing occurs in
~95–100% of human multiexon genes, but how much is functional is debated
(see Open questions).
Mechanism is becoming medicine. Splicing-factor mutations drive cancer and
genetic disease (splicing-and-disease); the first splicing-targeted drugs
are approved (splicing-therapeutics).

Open questions

Functionality of AS — ✅ Mostly reconciled (see
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate): the apparent
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative (functional) vs
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (noise) clash is largely a
difference in the definition of “functional”, the denominator counted, and the
evidence weighted — not a factual contradiction. Both agree on a conserved
functional core; residual uncertainty is the size of the low-abundance tail,
resolvable only with long-read/single-cell/CRISPR data.
Co- vs post-transcriptional timing trigger — mechanism unknown
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Interpretable, tissue-specific splicing-code models — still open
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).

Key concepts
spliceosome · alternative-splicing · splicing-code ·
post-transcriptional-splicing · splicing-and-disease ·
splicing-therapeutics — see index for the full catalog.

wiki/sources/capitanchik-2024-from-compu.title: capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the
wiki/sources/capitanchik-2024-from-compu.content:
From computational models of the splicing code to regulatory mechanisms and therapeutic implications
One-line: A review tracing 30 years of computational splicing-code models — from position weight matrices to deep-learning networks like SpliceAI and Pangolin — and how they illuminate splicing regulatory mechanisms and enable rational design of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

The splicing-code is written in genomic sequence: RNA sequences/structures within exons and introns that determine outcomes (largely via splicing-factors such as snRNAs and rna-binding-proteins) plus DNA sequences that couple splicing to transcription and chromatin. The “ideal” model would predict the full set of transcript isoforms and their frequencies from sequence alone in any cellular context, and approximate underlying biology.
Models evolved through stages: (a) models of core motifs (e.g. MaxEntScan scoring splice-sites); (b) integrative models using ~1,000+ predefined features (Splicing Code, SPANR); (c) end-to-end deep learning that learns features de novo from raw sequence (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice); (d) large omics-prediction models spanning whole loci (Borzoi, BigRNA); (e) self-supervised language models (SpliceBERT, Nucleotide Transformer).
Four core motifs form the minimal genetic code read by the spliceosome: 5′ splice site (consensus G|GU(A/G)AGU, recognized by U1 snRNP), branch point ((C/U)U(A/G)A(C/U)), polypyrimidine tract (bound by U2AF65), and 3′ splice site ((C/U)AG|, recognized by U2AF35). Weaker metazoan core motifs provide flexibility for alternative-splicing.
splicing-regulatory-elements (intronic/exonic enhancers and silencers) act position-dependently and multivalently; the same regulator can enhance or repress depending on binding position relative to splice sites. This combinatorial, cotranscriptional-splicing-coupled logic is what models must capture.
Deep models drive splicing-and-disease applications: SpliceAI and Pangolin predict non-coding cryptic-splicing variants (~10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations); benchmarking informs ClinGen clinical variant classification guidelines.
Splicing is a validated therapeutic target — FDA-approved nusinersen (SSO) and risdiplam (small molecule) promote SMN2 exon 7 inclusion in spinal muscular atrophy; deep models (BigRNA, SpliceAI) are beginning to aid rational design of splice-shifting oligos and splice-gated gene-therapy vectors.

Notable claims / data

Human exons: median length 122 nt (micro-exons as short as 3 nt). Human introns: 30 nt to >1 million nt, median 1,710 nt. Long introns have lower GC than flanking exons; short introns have exon-like high GC and occur in highly expressed genes.
SpliceAI is a 32-layer deep residual CNN trained on human GENCODE; Pangolin uses the same architecture but is trained on multiple species (human, rhesus, mouse, rat) and four tissues (heart, liver, brain, testis), adding tissue-specific splice-site strength outputs.
Context window matters: extending input from 80 nt → 400 nt raised SpliceAI top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95%; extending to 10,000 nt added a further 3–5%. SpliceAI accuracy on lncRNAs (lacking coding bias) was similar to mRNAs (top-k ~84%).
Tissue-specific prediction remains weak: median Spearman correlation with RNA-seq was 0.22 (MTSplice) and 0.43 (Pangolin). DeltaSplice (30,000 nt window, brain RNA-seq from 8 mammals) reached r = 0.69–0.71 on Vex-Seq vs 0.56 for SpliceAI/Pangolin.
Saturation-mutagenesis benchmarking showed all models (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice) predict exonic variants — especially distal ones — much worse than intronic variants: a key area for improvement.
Clinical benchmarks: SpliceAI AUC 0.959 on BRCA1 near-splice-site variants; up to 0.80 (proximal) / 0.95 (distal intronic) on ABCA4; different algorithms win in different genes (SpliceAI best on ABCA4, others better on MYBPC), motivating consensus/integrative models (CADD-Splice, AbSplice, SpliceVault).
Borzoi (built on Enformer) and BigRNA take up to ~0.5 Mb input and predict tissue-specific RNA-seq coverage plus other functional genomics (CAGE, DNase-seq, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, RBP binding); BigRNA reportedly beats SpliceAI on exon-skipping variants and can predict exon-inclusion-promoting SSOs.
In silico mutagenesis of SpliceAI revealed it indirectly predicts nucleosome positions, contributing to exon-boundary determination (splicing-and-chromatin link). Pangolin mutagenesis: mutations nearly always decrease splice-site usage, implying most exons are near-optimal for splicing accuracy.
Interpretability tools (enhanced integrated gradients, Deep SHAP, attention weights, nucleotide dependency maps) applied to models discovered new biology — e.g. EIG on liver exons identified A1CF as a novel liver-specific splicing regulator (later validated); an interpretable model found stem-loops and G-poor sequences drive exon skipping.

Connections

Central reference for splicing-code modelling; complements core-mechanism concepts: spliceosome, splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-factors, rna-binding-proteins, alternative-splicing.
splicing-and-chromatin: DeepCode found H3K36me3 and H3K79me1 most predictive; SF3B1 interacts with nucleosomes; H3K36me3 enriched in (especially constitutive) exons; SpliceAI implicitly learns nucleosome positions.
cotranscriptional-splicing / post-transcriptional-splicing: RNA Pol II elongation speed affects splicing; first-exon splicing needs cap binding complex; last-exon splicing couples to 3′-end cleavage/polyadenylation; splicing inhibits intronic poly(A) use.
splicing-and-evolution: multi-species training (Pangolin, DeltaSplice, SMsplice) leverages functional conservation; developmentally dynamic alternative-splicing events are most conserved across vertebrates; SMsplice found mammals rely more on regulatory elements, plants on core motifs/structure.
splicing-and-disease / splicing-therapeutics: variant diagnostics (ClinGen, ACMG/AMP), SMA (SMN2/nusinersen/risdiplam), Duchenne, ALS (TDP43 cryptic exons; TDP43–RAVER1 fusion proposal), splice-gated vectors designed with SpliceAI.

Open questions

How to close the gap between high black-box accuracy and mechanistic understanding (explainable-AI toolkit underused for splicing vs transcription); over-reliance on black-box models in the clinic is risky.
Context-dependent / tissue-specific alternative-splicing prediction is limited mainly by scarce training data (few alternative events in healthy-tissue RNA-seq; past focus on cassette exons narrowed data).
Predicting full-length transcript isoforms (not just per-splice-site usage) remains unsolved — needs long-read and uniform-coverage single-cell data (Nanopore, PacBio, Smart-Seq3, VASA-seq, SnISOr-Seq, scNanoGPS).
Need uniform processing, curation and sharing of CLIP/RNA-structure datasets (e.g. Flow, Snakemake/Nextflow workflows); crowdsourced challenges (CAGI, Kaggle/RibonanzaNet) could target alternative-splicing prediction.
Future: multimodal models linking splicing mechanisms (CLIP, RNA structure), outcomes (RNA-seq) and downstream RNA fate (half-life, translation efficiency) in one model.

wiki/sources/choquet-2025-the-regulation.title: choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of
wiki/sources/choquet-2025-the-regulation.content:
The regulation and function of post-transcriptional RNA splicing
One-line: A review arguing that up to ~40% of mammalian introns are spliced post-transcriptionally — largely while transcripts remain chromatin-associated after polyadenylation — and that the timing of splicing is itself a major, regulated layer of gene expression control.
Key takeaways

splicing occurs both co-transcriptionally (during transcription) and post-transcriptionally (after transcription completes); recent long-read and subcellular methods reveal the timing axis with high spatial/temporal resolution. The spliceosome assembly steps are presumed identical for both modes, but it is unknown whether the post-transcriptional delay occurs before or during early assembly, or what triggers eventual splicing.
Roughly 27–40% of mammalian introns are present at high levels in polyadenylated, chromatin-associated RNA, and >70% of expressed genes carry at least one post-transcriptionally retained intron. This makes chromatin-associated post-transcriptional splicing a common feature of gene expression.
Polyadenylated transcripts can stay on chromatin long after synthesis ends; splicing completion appears to be a rate-limiting step for transcript release from chromatin, controlling when mRNA becomes available for nuclear export and translation.
Most retained introns are eventually excised productively rather than degraded; nuclear decay (via the nuclear exosome / PABPN1 hyperadenylation pathway) is the minority fate, affecting only ~4% of protein-coding genes (PUND transcripts).
Detained introns are a specialized class held in the nucleus until specific signals trigger removal — a regulatory reservoir used in neurons, spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation, muscle stem cells and heat-shock responses.
Splicing timing is shaped by cis features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary structure) and trans factors/RBPs (PTBP1, SR proteins, U2 snRNA, SNIP1), with phosphorylation (CLK/SRPK), methylation (PRMT5, PRMT1/4) and m6A as key modulators.
Introns flanking alternative exons are preferentially spliced post-transcriptionally, plausibly buying time for correct splice-site choice; Pol II elongation rate and co-transcriptional RNA folding feed into these outcomes.

Notable claims / data

Up to 40% of mammalian introns are retained after transcription termination; transcriptome-wide estimates put post-transcriptional splicing at 30–45% of introns (Table 1). Most mammalian introns (~70%) are excised on chromatin.
Subcellular TimeLapse-seq (K562): mRNAs have a median chromatin half-life of ~50 min, some genes >170 min — often exceeding transcription time itself; transcripts spend most of their nuclear life on chromatin, not free in nucleoplasm.
Microscopy: an intron in a β-globin reporter was excised ~10-fold faster and more frequently (87%) after release from the transcription site; for 6 of 10 endogenous human introns, post-transcriptional excision was more probable than co-transcriptional.
Detained introns tend to be shorter, GC-rich, have weaker 5′/3′ splice sites, and are more conserved; ~31% of nuclear-speckle-associated retained introns overlap annotated detained introns vs 12% for lamina-associated ones.
Species differences: Drosophila shows higher splicing completion on chromatin than mouse liver; in Arabidopsis >40% of chromatin RNA is polyadenylated, ~1/3 partially spliced — similar to mammals.
67% of introns flanking alternative exons are excised post-transcriptionally vs 30% of constitutive introns in mESCs.
~20% of human genes have ≥1 intronic poly(A) (PAS) event. Terminal-intron excision is stimulated by 3′-end cleavage/poly(A) signal and may require an optimal poly(A) tail length (PABPN1).
Disease: PRMT5 is aberrantly activated in glioblastoma, excising detained introns in cell-cycle genes to drive proliferation — a hijacked developmental program; PRMT5 inhibition suppresses tumour growth (therapeutic vulnerability). FUS autoregulation via intron retention is disrupted by ALS-causing nuclear-localization-signal mutations.

Connections

Definitive reference for post-transcriptional-splicing and its contrast with cotranscriptional-splicing; central to splicing-and-chromatin (chromatin retention of polyadenylated pre-mRNA, nuclear speckles vs lamina).
Ties alternative-splicing timing to Pol II elongation rate and co-transcriptional RNA structure.
Mechanistic links to splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins, CLK/SRPK, PRMTs, SNIP1, U2 snRNA), and to disease/therapeutics via PRMT5 in glioblastoma and FUS in ALS.
splicing-and-evolution: detained introns and intronic PAS show elevated cross-species conservation, implying selected regulatory roles.

Open questions

What signals trigger the eventual post-transcriptional splicing reaction, and is the delay pre- or post-engagement with the spliceosome?
What regulatory features sort partially spliced chromatin-associated transcripts toward productive splicing vs nuclear degradation?
How do nuclear speckles mechanistically couple transcript localization to splicing timing?
To what degree is m6A a universal splicing modulator vs a context-specific one (most marks are not near splice sites and are added post-transcriptionally)?
Do exons regulated by transcription rate overlap with those regulated by delayed removal of flanking introns?
Can post-transcriptional splicing be therapeutically targeted in diseases where it is dysregulated, e.g. cancers?

wiki/sources/editorial-2025-rna-splicing.title: editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer
wiki/sources/editorial-2025-rna-splicing.content:
RNA splicing — a central layer of gene regulation
One-line: A framing editorial introducing a Nature Reviews online collection, surveying how recent technological and computational advances (cryo-EM, sequencing, machine learning) have deepened understanding of splicing as a central layer of gene regulation.

Note: This is a short framing editorial introducing a journal collection across Nature Reviews Genetics and Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology. It is best used as an overview/orientation piece rather than a primary research source.

Key takeaways

Most eukaryotic genes are organized as a “mosaic” of introns and exons; splicing removes introns and ligates exons to produce mature mRNA, and is essential for accurate translation of DNA sequence information.
The spliceosome is a dynamic ribonucleoprotein complex of five snRNAs and numerous proteins that recognizes conserved motifs at exon–intron boundaries and carries out a two-step transesterification reaction; recent cryo-EM has resolved multiple conformational states.
alternative-splicing (regulated inclusion/exclusion of exons) greatly expands the coding potential of the genome, producing isoforms that affect mRNA stability, localization and translation.
Timing matters: splicing occurs cotranscriptional-splicing or post-transcriptional-splicing, and depends on nuclear compartmentalization via biomolecular condensates such as nuclear speckles and paraspeckles, which concentrate splicing-factors.
