Genetic variation disrupting splice-sites or splicing-regulatory-elements underlies splicing-and-disease; splicing-therapeutics (antisense oligonucleotides, small molecules) is an increasingly validated treatment strategy.

Notable claims / data

More than 95% of human multi-intron genes undergo alternative-splicing.
“Split genes” were discovered in 1977 (Richard Roberts and Phillip Sharp), earning the 1993 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.
The spliceosome comprises five small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs) plus numerous associated proteins.
Spliceosome catalysis is a two-step transesterification reaction.
Genetic variants can cause exon skipping, intron retention, or activation of cryptic splice sites; mutations in core spliceosomal proteins can compromise splicing fidelity or efficiency.
The splicing-code is defined as “a set of rules, an algorithm or a computational model that could predict the splice isoforms, and their frequencies, produced from any transcribed gene in a specific cellular context,” encompassing cis-regulatory elements, RNA structure and trans-acting contributors.

Connections

splicing-therapeutics: ASOs and small molecules applied to genetic disorders, neurodegenerative diseases and certain cancers.
splicing-code / machine learning: deep learning is now a powerful tool for modelling the code and predicting variant effects on splicing, though interpretability remains a challenge; this can guide drug development and clinical diagnostics.
splicing-and-evolution: long-read sequencing reveals striking differences in alternative splicing extent between eukaryotes, and heritable variation in alternative-splicing patterns may contribute to adaptive evolutionary change.
splicing-and-chromatin / transcription: long-read sequencing and imaging illuminate splicing timing and its dynamic interplay with transcription and RNA processing.
rna-binding-proteins / splicing-factors organized within phase-separated condensates regulate splicing.

Open questions

How to improve the interpretability of deep-learning models of the splicing code.
How splicing shapes phenotypic outcomes and contributes to human disease (the editorial frames researchers as “poised to further decode” this).
The full regulatory logic linking splicing timing, nuclear compartmentalization, and isoform output.

wiki/sources/lee-2015-mechanisms-and-reg.title: lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative
wiki/sources/lee-2015-mechanisms-and-reg.content:
Mechanisms and Regulation of Alternative Pre-mRNA Splicing
One-line: A broad review of how the spliceosome assembles and catalyzes intron removal as a ribozyme, how alternative-splicing is regulated by cis-elements, RNA-binding proteins, RNA structure, and chromatin, and how splicing defects cause disease and offer therapeutic targets.
Key takeaways

splicing removes introns via the spliceosome, a ~4.8 MDa dynamic RNP machine of five snRNPs (U1, U2, U4/U6, U5) that assembles stepwise (E → A → B → Bact/B* → C) on every intron and recycles.
The spliceosome is a ribozyme: the catalytic center is RNA (the U2–U6 snRNA base-paired complex), structurally and mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns; metal-ion-rescue experiments in yeast U6 snRNA show RNA, not the prp8 protein, coordinates the catalytic divalent metals.
Alternative splicing scales with organismal complexity and expands the proteome; recent RNA-seq estimates indicate >95–100% of human multiexon genes produce at least two isoforms (see numbers below).
alternative-splicing is set up by cis-acting splicing-regulatory-elements (exonic/intronic enhancers and silencers) bound by splicing-factors — SR proteins (activators) and hnRNPs (repressors), plus tissue-specific rna-binding-proteins (Nova, PTB, Rbfox, Muscleblind/CELF) whose effect (activator vs. repressor) is position-dependent.
Splice-site choice can also be dictated by RNA–RNA base pairing (Drosophila DSCAM docking/selector sites) and, in trans, by a snoRNA (HBII-52) regulating the serotonin receptor 2C transcript.
splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks, “adapter” proteins (CHD1–U2 snRNP; MRG15–PTB), and RNA polymerase II elongation speed couple chromatin state to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing outcomes; some transcripts are spliced post-transcriptional-splicing.
splicing-and-disease: mutations at splice-sites or in silencers/enhancers cause disease (β-globin thalassemia, SMN2/spinal muscular atrophy, ALS via hnRNPA1/TDP-43/FUS prion-like domains); recurrent somatic mutations in 3′-splice-site factors drive myeloid cancers.
splicing-therapeutics: small molecules (spliceostatin targeting SF3b), antisense oligonucleotides (TSUNAMI correcting SMN2), and CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing are emerging interventions.

Notable claims / data

Human spliceosome: ~4.8 MDa, 40–60S; >200 proteins in metazoans, ~100 in yeast. Transition B → Bact loses U1/U4 snRNAs and ~35 proteins while adding 12; Bact → C loses 2 proteins and adds 9.
Average human gene: ~8 exons, 7 introns, ≥3 alternatively spliced isoforms; RNA-seq estimates average ~7 mRNA isoforms per gene.
Ensembl (pre-RNA-seq) alternative-splicing fractions of multiexon genes: C. elegans 25%, Drosophila 45%, mouse 63%, human 88%; ~63% of mouse genes vs. >95–100% of human genes show alternative splicing.
Drosophila DSCAM can generate >36,000 isoforms (~3× the fly gene count); exon 6 cluster has 48 mutually exclusive exons selected by docking/selector RNA base pairing, with hnRNP hrp36 enforcing mutual exclusivity.
Cancer: recurrent somatic mutations in U2AF1 (U2AF35), SRSF2 (SC35), SF3B1 (SAP155), ZRSR2 (URP) in myelodysplastic syndrome/CLL; SRSF1 (ASF/SF2) overexpression acts as an oncogene in mice (mTORC1-pathway targets).
U1 snRNP is the most abundant snRNP (~1 million copies/cell) and suppresses premature cleavage and polyadenylation (“telescripting”); blocking U1 causes premature polyadenylation within ~1 kb of transcription start sites.
RNA pol II elongation ~3–4 kb/min; “slow” polymerase mutants alter splicing patterns.
Structural milestones cited: U1 snRNP reconstituted/crystallized (5.5 Å; native 4.4 Å), U6 snRNP core (1.7 Å), U4 snRNP core; prp8 has RNase H- and reverse-transcriptase-like domains near the active site.
Caveat: deep sequencing may detect low-abundance isoforms that could be spliceosome errors rather than functional products.

Connections

Foundational support for spliceosome, splicing, and alternative-splicing as a single integrated topic; ties splicing-factors and splicing-regulatory-elements to the position-dependent splicing-code (Nova RNA map).
Links splicing-and-chromatin and cotranscriptional-splicing via RNA pol II speed and histone marks; connects post-transcriptional-splicing for slow/long introns.
Connects splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics (cancer splicing-factor mutations → SF3b-targeting drugs; SMN2 → antisense).
Supports splicing-and-evolution: spliceosome evolved from group II self-splicing introns; alternative splicing correlates with organismal complexity.

Open questions

Which specific “causal” mis-spliced mRNAs (not just global pattern shifts) drive particular cancer phenotypes?
How prevalent is trans-acting small-RNA / Argonaute control of alternative splicing beyond HBII-52, given nuclear Ago-2 binds chromatin and splicing targets?
How much detected low-abundance alternative splicing is functional versus spliceosomal noise?
How does in vivo transcriptome RNA structure (SHAPE/DMS probing) shape splicing, and how do disease mutations disrupt it?

wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.title: marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative
wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.content:
The physiology of alternative splicing
One-line: A review arguing that most of the transcriptomic complexity generated by alternative-splicing in multicellular eukaryotes is reflected at the protein level and shapes diverse physiological and pathological processes, from tissue differentiation to thermal regulation, cancer and approved splicing-correcting therapies.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing occurs in ~95% of human genes and is “more a rule than an exception” in multicellular organisms; the central question is how much is functional versus noise. The authors argue most is functional, citing four decades of single-gene studies.
The apparent contradiction between sensitive RNA-seq and low-sensitivity mass spectrometry (which suggested ~85% of alternative exons are irrelevant) is attributed to technical limits of splicing proteomics (“absence of evidence is not evidence of absence”); ribosome profiling shows ~75% of medium/high-abundance skipped-exon mRNAs are ribosome-engaged.
cotranscriptional-splicing: splice-site choice depends on cis-elements (splicing-regulatory-elements) AND trans splicing-factors AND coupling to Pol II elongation. Kinetic coupling model — slow elongation promotes inclusion of class I exons and skipping of class II exons. splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks (H3K9ac, H3K9me2) modulate elongation and thus splicing outcomes.
Global alternative-splicing changes drive cell proliferation, cancer (splicing-and-disease), ASD and thermal regulation; gene-specific switches drive neuron self-avoidance, infrared sensing, telomere maintenance and the Warburg effect.
splicing-therapeutics: mastering splicing control yielded clinically approved ASO/small-molecule drugs for SMA (nusinersen, risdiplam) and DMD (eteplirsen).
splicing-and-evolution: alternative splicing per se has adaptive value as a readily available pool of mRNA variability that can be co-opted without eliminating gene functions or making new genes; it prevails in K-selected multicellular eukaryotes.

Notable claims / data

Drosophila Dscam can generate up to 38,000 mRNA variants (19,000 distinct extracellular domains) — more than the ~15,000 genes in the fly genome — via mutually exclusive exons in 4 regions; basis of stochastic neurite self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion. Vertebrates achieve self-avoidance differently, via clustered protocadherin promoters (~60 variants), no splicing.
CLK1 is a body-temperature sensor: activity is >5-fold higher at 35 °C than 38 °C; its 25-aa activation domain becomes flexible at high temperature (domain-swap with temperature-insensitive SRPKs confirms the activation domain is the thermosensor). Drives global SR-protein phosphorylation and splicing. Conserved in poikilotherms (alligators, turtles, Drosophila); links to fly circadian tim siesta behavior.
Warburg effect: PKM E9/E10 mutually exclusive exons (each 56 aa) → PKM1 (constitutive tetramer) vs PKM2 (low-activity, favors anabolic intermediates, cancer/embryonic). Regulated by hnRNPA1, hnRNPA2, PTBP1, whose genes are activated by MYC.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) infrared sensing: trigeminal-ganglion-specific TRPV1 splice variant (E14a, truncated C-terminus) lowers heat threshold from 43 °C to 30 °C — “sees” prey while retaining noxious-heat protection.
Telomeres: hTERT E2 skipping (vs SON-promoted inclusion) is a developmental switch controlling telomerase; an individual with a SON mutation had shorter telomeres.
ASD: neural microexons (3–27 nt, usually multiples of 3) are highly conserved, promoted by SRRM4 (reduced in ASD); examples in eIF4G1/eIF4G3 and CPEB4 (microexon 4). Gut-microbiota transplant from ASD donors into germ-free mice altered >500 splicing events (52 autism-related) with few gene-expression changes.
Cancer: SRSF1 (SF2/ASF) is a proto-oncogene; SRSF2/U2AF1/SF3B1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes; PRMT5 methylates SRSF1 (AML target) and removes detained introns.
SMA: SMN1/SMN2 differ in 11 nt (key C>T in SMN2 E7 causes skipping). Nusinersen (FDA 2016/EMA 2017) blocks intron-7 ISS bound by hnRNPA1/A2 → E7 inclusion; first splicing-corrective drug. Marasco et al. found SMN2 E7 is a class II exon, and the ASO paradoxically induces H3K9me2 — HDAC inhibitors (VPA) synergize by relieving the elongation roadblock. Risdiplam = small molecule affecting snRNA–5′SS pairing.
DMD: E49–E50 deletions = 13% of cases; eteplirsen (30-mer ASO) targets an ESE in E51 to force E51 skipping, restoring frame (Becker-like internally truncated dystrophin).
Textbook example CALCA: one gene → calcitonin (thyroid C cells, E4 skipped) vs CGRP1 (neurons, E4 included) via alternative splicing + cleavage/polyadenylation + cell-specific proteolysis. FOXP1 mutually exclusive E18/E18b switches between differentiation and pluripotency (FOXP1-ES), upstream-controlled by MBNL1/MBNL2.

Connections

Vertebrate organ transcriptomes: alternative-splicing profiles track species identity more than organ type (primates highest complexity); PTBP1 isoform reshapes splicing across species — links to splicing-and-evolution.
Coupling of alternative-splicing to NMD: ~15,000 human variants annotated as NMD targets (Ensembl 2019); post-transcriptional-splicing control of protein output.
Tissue/organ-specific: RBM20/titin in dilated cardiomyopathy; endothelial flow; obesity; pancreatic circadian exocytosis — rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors as drivers of homeostasis and splicing-and-disease.
G9a (EHMT2) E10 inclusion (Kornblihtt group) creates an H3K9me2-driven positive feedback loop reinforcing neuronal differentiation — direct tie to splicing-and-chromatin.

Open questions

How much RNA-seq-detected isoform diversity is truly functional vs too low-abundance to matter? Authors suggest long-read (third-generation) sequencing to resolve the transcriptomic/proteomic discrepancy.
Why do mutations in constitutive splicing-factors (U2AF1, SF3B1) preferentially perturb specific alternative events rather than global splicing? (Partly redundancy; partly late-recruited regulatory roles of core spliceosome components.)
Molecular mechanism of the bat TRPV1 dorsal-root vs trigeminal splicing switch remains unknown.
Whether ASD gut-microbiota metabolites reverse brain splicing changes, and whether HDAC inhibitors also boost risdiplam efficacy, remain to be tested.

wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.title: peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes
wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.content:
Emerging role of G-quadruplexes in alternative splicing regulation
One-line: A mechanistic review arguing that RNA G-quadruplexes (rG4s) are an emerging class of secondary-structure regulators of alternative-splicing, acting position-dependently near splice-sites to recruit or block splicing factors, with strong links to cancer and neurodegeneration and a therapeutic angle via G4 ligands.
Key takeaways

g-quadruplex structures are noncanonical four-stranded folds in guanine-rich nucleic acid: guanines pair via Hoogsteen (not Watson–Crick) bonds into stacked G-quartets, stabilised by K+ cations. They form in both DNA (dG4s) and RNA (rG4s) and act as rna-structure-and-splicing elements distinct from sequence motifs.
rG4s regulate alternative-splicing in a position-dependent way: located near donor (5’ ss) or acceptor (3’ ss) sites, they either recruit rG4-binding splicing-factors that redirect the spliceosome to proximal sites, or remodel pre-mRNA to mask/expose splice sites — functioning as enhancers or silencers (splicing-regulatory-elements).
This is a structure-based complement to the splicing-code: rG4s overlay sequence-defined splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements, and their effect depends on dynamic folding equilibria rather than a fixed consensus.
Many examples span all five major ASE types (exon skipping, intron retention, alternative 5’/3’ ss, mutually exclusive exons); rna-binding-proteins including the hnRNP family, FMRP, AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25 read these rG4s.
dG4s also influence splicing indirectly via cotranscriptional-splicing: by slowing RNA Pol II elongation (and via R-loops on the non-template strand), they alter exon inclusion (Box 3), separate from the direct rG4 effects.
rG4-controlled splicing is heavily implicated in splicing-and-disease, especially cancer (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x, BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) and neurodegeneration (BACE1/Alzheimer, FMR1/Fragile X).
G4 ligands (e.g. PhenDC3, pyridostatin, GQC-05) are model splicing-therapeutics that stabilise/unfold rG4s or block G4-binding proteins; effects across ASEs are often contradictory, so no general rule yet exists.

Notable claims / data

Scale: ~100,000 proteins from ~20,000 human genes via AS. ~60% of human transcripts contain ≥1 putative quadruplex-forming sequence (PQS); ~1,200,000 PQSs (300,000 unique motifs) across transcripts and splice junctions; canonical folding rule d(GGG+N1–7)x4 predicts ~400,000 dG4s in the genome.
A threefold enrichment of G4s near splice junctions, preferentially on the non-template (coding) strand (implying the corresponding rG4 matters); G4-at-ss enrichment is conserved in vertebrates but not invertebrates (possibly because AS matters less there).
FMR1 (Fragile X): two rG4s in exon 15 inhibit inclusion; FMRP binds them via its RGG motif and modulates its own splicing, favouring minor ISO2/ISO3 isoforms (autoregulatory feedback). ~15 loop mutations (ΔG4) altered exon-15 splicing without changing translation.
AFF family (AFF1–4; AFF2/FMR2 → FRAXE intellectual disability) bind rG4s via a conserved C-terminal domain (not RGG), localise to nuclear speckles, and broadly modulate AS.
CD44: intron-8 rG4 (element I-8) promotes the epithelial CD44v isoform; hnRNPF regulates it (depletion accelerates EMT). Alkaloids emetine and cephaeline modulate inclusion by unfolding rG4s (~60% of emetine-affected ASEs are skipped exons).
EWS-FLI (Ewing sarcoma): exon-8 rG4 promotes exon exclusion (keeping the oncoprotein); hnRNPH1 destabilises the rG4. Pyridostatin (PDS) blocks hnRNPH1 binding and reduces viability of EWSR1-fusion cells.
BCL-2: exon-3 rG4 favours skipping → proapoptotic BCL-2β; interplay with three nearby m6A sites; SRSF1 (an rG4-binding factor) resolves the rG4. Bcl-2β sensitises breast cancer cells to chemotherapy.
hTERT: intron-6 rG4s act as intronic splicing silencers; ligands Bisquinolinium 12459, CX-5461, and PhenDC3 shift splicing toward the inactive β transcript (telomestatin/BRACO19 had no effect — ligand selectivity).
TP53: intron-3 rG4 promotes intron-2 retention → dominant-negative ΔNp53/Δ40p53; ligand 360A shifts toward full-length p53. A common intronic polymorphism disrupting this rG4 is linked to increased cancer risk.
BCL-x (“rG4 saga”, Figure 1): exon-2 GQ-2/Q2 rG4 (overlaps the Gt16 G-tract) downstream of the alternative 5’ ss controls Bcl-xL (antiapoptotic) vs Bcl-xS (proapoptotic) balance. Splicing factor RBM25 binds Q2 directly via its arginine–glutamate (RE) domain — a novel G4-binding domain — and this binding is required for Bcl-xS production. Ligands GQC-05, PhenDC3, and the novel PhenDH8/PhenDH9 promote Bcl-xS; a genome screen for the Gt16-like G-tract found 3374 exons in 2707 genes.
Other cases: PAX9 intron-1 rG4 (positively regulates splicing; conserved in placental mammals; affects dentition — splicing-and-evolution); IGFN1 intron-15 (renal/breast cancer, PDS); CD133 introns 3/7 (cancer stem cells, RHPS4); BACE1 exon-3 alternative 5’ ss (hnRNPH/hnRNPL; Alzheimer); TRα/THRA alternative 3’ ss (G30 element, only suggestive evidence); vertebrate mirtron 5’-arm rG4s aiding mirtron biogenesis.
Mechanism (Box 6, splicing-and-chromatin/condensate angle): rG4–protein interactions may nucleate liquid–liquid phase separation (LLPS) at nuclear speckles, concentrating splicing factors — proposed but not yet demonstrated for the validated rG4 cases.

Connections

Structure vs sequence: extends splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements from a sequence-motif view to a secondary-structure view — rG4s are folded ESE/ISE/ESS/ISS-like elements whose activity is position-dependent and dynamic, complementing the splicing-code.
Sits in the same RNA-structure thread as Lee 2015 (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative), which discusses RNA secondary structure masking splice sites; this review supplies the G4-specific molecular detail and the new [[rna-structure-and-splicing]] entity.
splicing-and-disease: a curated cancer-centric catalogue (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x/BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) plus neurodegeneration (BACE1, FMR1).
splicing-therapeutics: positions G4 ligands as splicing modulators; relates to BH3-mimetic (venetoclax) and SF3B1-modulator strategies, and to G4-inducing ASOs (G-ASOs) for exon skipping in Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
cotranscriptional-splicing: dG4/R-loop effects on Pol II elongation (Box 3) tie G4 biology to the transcription–splicing coupling thread.

Open questions

The molecular mechanisms of rG4-mediated AS, and the role of phase separation, remain largely unproven (mostly hypothesised models).
Which methods can confirm that an in-silico PQS actually forms a functional rG4 in cellulo and controls a specific gene’s AS (single-RNA-molecule monitoring is lacking).
G4 ligand selectivity is a major limitation: hundreds of thousands of competing dG4s/rG4s exist, effects across ASEs are often antinomic, and no general predictive “G4–ligand code” yet exists.
Is rG4-mediated splicing a general feature of apoptosis-regulating genes with opposing isoforms (e.g. BCL-x, MCL-1)?
Systematic identification of the full set of rG4-binding RBPs that reshape the AS landscape, and the influence of surrounding chemical RNA modifications (e.g. m6A), are still needed.

wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.title: rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing
wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.content:
Regulation of pre-mRNA splicing: roles in physiology and disease, and therapeutic prospects
One-line: A review of recent advances in the molecular mechanisms that distinguish introns from exons and regulate alternative-splicing, connecting these to human disease and to the first generation of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

Intron removal is carried out by the spliceosome, comprising 5 snRNP complexes (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) plus more than 150 additional proteins, through a two-step transesterification reaction; cryo-EM has now captured snapshots of splicing at many assembly and catalytic steps. A principle of accuracy is that intron boundaries are recognized multiple times.
splice-sites in higher eukaryotes are remarkably diverse and often resemble non-splice-site sequences, so recognition depends on base pairing of splicing-factors/snRNPs plus context; U1 snRNA base-pairing defines 5’ss “strength”, and BP/PPT/3’ss recognition by SF1, U2AF1/U2AF2 and U2 snRNP (SF3B1/PHF5A) defines the 3’ end.
splicing-regulatory-elements (ESE/ESS, ISE/ISS) recruited by rna-binding-proteins (SR proteins, hnRNPs, RBM, CELF) tune splice-site recognition and exon definition; the same motif can act positively or negatively depending on position (the splicing-code).
Mutations in pre-mRNAs and core splicing-factors drive splicing-and-disease: genetic disorders, neurodegeneration and cancer (SF3B1, U2AF1/2, U1 snRNA mutations).
splicing-therapeutics are now clinical reality: antisense oligonucleotides (nusinersen, eteplirsen), the small molecule risdiplam, engineered U1 snRNAs, and SF3B1/RBM39-targeting drugs in oncology.
cotranscriptional-splicing is widespread but not universal; Pol II elongation rate, splicing-and-chromatin (histone marks, nucleosome positioning, CTD), nuclear compartmentalization, RNA modifications and RNA structure all shape outcomes — alongside post-transcriptional-splicing (e.g. detained introns).

Notable claims / data

Massively parallel assay of all 32,768 possible NNN/GYNNNN 5’ss sequences confirmed U1-base-pairing relevance but revealed strong context effects; ~90% of BRCA2 5’ss mutations in breast cancer affect splicing. G at +1 is required for catalysis; pyrimidine at +2 present in 99.6% of 5’ss.
Spinal muscular atrophy: caused by loss of SMN1; nusinersen (approved 2016) blocks an ISS to boost SMN2 exon 7 inclusion via quarterly intrathecal injection. Risdiplam (FDA approval 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone, “repairs” the U1–SMN2 exon 7 bulge by aiding U1C zinc-finger contact.
Familial dysautonomia: IKBKAP intron 20 +6 mutation causes exon skipping; therapeutic strategies include ASOs, modified U1 snRNAs, kinetin, and RECTAS (enhances SRSF6 phosphorylation).
SF3B1 mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts, 15–36% of eye melanomas, and are among the most common single mutations in CLL; K700E correlates with worse prognosis in CLL but better in MDS. SF3B1 mutations activate cryptic 3’ss 10–30 nt upstream and converge on repressing tumour suppressor BRD9 via a “poison exon”.
U2AF1 S34F/Y vs Q157P/R mutants change which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected. SF3B1 inhibitors (H3B-8800, in trials; pladienolide B; spliceostatin A; sudemycins) block the open→closed BP-recognition conformational change; indisulam degrades RBM39 (synthetic lethality).
Splicing modulators generate tumour neoantigens, combinable with anti-PD1 checkpoint blockade. Eteplirsen targets ESEs to skip a mutant dystrophin exon in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, restoring an in-frame shorter functional protein. Pladienolide B reprograms mouse pluripotent cells to totipotent blastomere-like cells.
Two-thirds of all possible mutations in an alternative exon affect inclusion (vs constitutive exons). Internal exons are constrained to ~50–250 nt by exon definition; microexons (~3–27 nt) use SRRM4-coordinated recognition relevant to autism.
m6A regulates splicing: at 3’ss AG it inhibits U2AF1 binding (SAM-synthetase feedback in C. elegans and via METTL16 in humans); readers YTHDC1 (recruits SRSF3, antagonizes SRSF10) and hnRNPG/RBMX modulate exon inclusion.

Connections

alternative-splicing diversifies the proteome and regulates expression via PTC introduction and NMD (~30% of AS events introduce PTCs); links to splicing-and-evolution (joint evolution of splicing and protein codes; circular RNAs from back splicing).
splicing-code prediction: decades of tools from SpliceSiteFinder (1987) through SpliceAI, MMSplice, Pangolin; saturation mutagenesis and AI methods are the path forward.
splicing-and-chromatin: MRG15 links H3K36me3 to PTB recruitment; genome-edited histone marks induce splicing changes during EMT.

Open questions

How widespread is the functional relevance of alternative-splicing versus transcriptome noise, and how much variability exists cell-to-cell?
Do introns have autonomous functions beyond being removed by-products?
How general is cotranscriptional-splicing, and do cell-type- or substrate-specific spliceosomes exist?
How can splice sites be reliably predicted from genomic sequence, and how do lncRNAs contribute to splicing regulation?

wiki/sources/sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-t.title: sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying
wiki/sources/sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-t.content:
Tools and tactics for studying alternative splicing
One-line: A methods/tools review of the modern toolkit for studying alternative-splicing — short- and long-read sequencing (bulk, single-cell, spatial), CRISPR-based functional assays, minigene/reporter assays, population/sQTL genetics, and deep-learning splicing-code models — organized around the goals of building a complete mRNA-isoform atlas, testing isoform function, and decoding the genetic control of splicing. Complements the wiki’s biology-focused sources by cataloguing how splicing is actually measured and perturbed.
Key takeaways

splicing-methods for alternative-splicing have evolved through a timeline (Fig. 2): splicing/AS discovery (1977) → RT-PCR (1989) → ESTs (1991) → exon-junction microarrays (2003) → high-throughput RNA-seq (2008) → long-read full-length sequencing (2013) and single-cell isoform RNA-seq → spatially resolved full-length mRNA sequencing (2023). Two landmark RNA-seq studies estimated 92-95% of human multi-exon genes undergo AS.
AS forms catalogued: cassette-exon inclusion/skipping, alternative 5’/3’ splice-sites (exon elongation/truncation), intron retention, plus alternative TSS and polyadenylation; these are frequently coordinated, and long-read data show coordinated/independent splicing of distant exons and that intron-removal order need not follow transcription order (cotranscriptional-splicing).
Short-read RNA-seq analysis splits into isoform-based vs count-based (exon-based and event-based) methods; event-based tools compute per cent spliced-in (PSI) and compare ΔPSI. Splice-aware aligners (STAR, HISAT2), pseudo-aligners (Salmon, Kallisto) and de novo assemblers (Trinity, Oases, SPLASH) are standard. Benchmarks show substantial tool-to-tool discrepancy → orthogonal validation needed.
Long-read platforms (PacBio SMRT/HiFi, ONT) directly capture full-length isoforms and exon connectivity, resolving complex patterns short reads cannot; ONT direct RNA-seq additionally reads native RNA + modifications (m6A) without RT/PCR.
CRISPR-based functional assays directly test isoform function: Cas9 splice-site/exon editing and pooled fitness screens, base editors for precise splice-site/enhancer disruption, and Cas13/dCasRx for RNA-level, isoform-specific, reversible perturbation and SRE mapping (splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-factors).
Genetic control is mapped via sQTL analysis, allele-specific expression (ASE), and massively parallel reporter assays (MPRAs); a recent GTEx v8 analysis found a substantial fraction of trait/disease variants act through splicing.
Deep-learning splicing-code models progressed from feature-based predictors to CNNs (spliceai, Pangolin) and newer multimodal/transformer/foundation models (DeltaSplice, Borzoi, DNABERT, SpliceBERT, AlphaGenome); none yet capture full cell-type/condition-specific isoform repertoires.
Central open debate: distinguishing functional isoforms from pervasive “splicing noise” — many AS isoforms are low-abundance and may be nonfunctional mis-splicing, but a subset is biologically meaningful.

Notable claims / data

Platforms (Table 2): Illumina >99.9% base accuracy, reads 50-300 bp; PacBio HiFi ~99.8% accuracy, 10-25 kb reads; ONT cDNA reads 10-20 kb avg (>1 Mb possible), ONT direct RNA ~1-1.3 kb avg (some >20 kb). PacBio/ONT continuous reads average 10-20 kb.
Library prep matters: rRNA is ~80% of total RNA; polyA selection generally preferred over rRNA depletion for splicing; UMIs remove PCR duplicates; strand-specific and paired-end improve junction calling. Typical AS depth 30-60M reads/sample; 100-200M for low-abundance isoforms or poorly annotated genomes.
Short-read limit: <300 bp reads vs ~3 kb average human mRNA → poor full-length reconstruction.
Large isoform-atlas efforts: ONT RNA-seq of GTEx samples found >70,000 novel transcripts; PacBio added to ENCODE4 to catalogue isoforms across 81 human/mouse samples (preprint). Ouro-Seq built a mouse single-cell long-read splicing atlas of >100,000 cells across 12 organs.
Single-cell: droplet 3’/5’ methods (10x Chromium) capture only transcript ends — insufficient for junctions; full-length plate methods (Smart-seq2/3, RamDA-seq, VASA-seq, FLASH-seq) enable event-level AS in single cells. Single-cell long-read methods: ScISOr-Seq2, scTaILoR-seq (>1,000 genes), HIT-scISOseq, MAS-ISO-seq, Ouro-Seq, snISOr-Seq. m6Anet quantifies m6A from ONT direct RNA.
Targeted long-read: nanopore amplicon sequencing resolved ~8,000 full-length cDNAs from four Drosophila genes incl. Dscam1 (most alternatively spliced gene known); RNA CaptureSeq enriches selected transcripts.
Spatial: MERFISH/seqFISH (imaging), barcoding methods (original 2016 spatial transcriptomics, Slide-seq, Seq-Scope, Stereo-seq, Stereo-cell); Spl-ISO-Seq did spatial long-read isoforms at 10-µm resolution (human cortex > white matter splicing regulation); Longcell pipeline handles single-cell/spatial Nanopore barcode/UMI errors.
Proteomics: most shotgun workflows use trypsin and miss splice-unique peptides; a study using six proteases + deep LC-MS found most frame-preserving AS isoforms are translated. Ribosome profiling and polyribosome long-read RNA-seq probe which isoforms are translated; nanopore protein sequencing emerging for proteoforms.
Functional-prediction tools: NEASE (pathway/PPI interpretation of AS), FAScore (ML predictor of functional exon-skipping), ORF cloning for high-throughput isoform interaction profiling (alternative isoforms share <half their binding partners). AlphaFold3 enables AS structural impact studies.
CRISPR examples: sgRNAs at MPRIP exon 23 splice sites → skipping → metastasis; targeting an RBFOX2 motif → inclusion → metastasis suppression (pancreatic cancer). Base-editor screen showed TAF5 exon 8 AS affects transcription. SpliceRUSH = high-throughput dCas13 SRE mapping; dCasRx fused to splicing factors (e.g. dCasRx-RBM25) activates exon inclusion at scale.
Reporter assays: splicing reporters tested >2 million random sequences for splice-site selection (predicts variants in SMN1, SMN2, CFTR, BRCA2); an MPRA of ~5,000 exonic disease mutations found ~10% disrupt splicing vs only 3% of common SNPs. EXSISERS quantifies endogenous protein isoforms (e.g. MAPT exon 10) via intein splicing in patient iPS cells.
sQTL/ASE tools: scNanoGPS (genotype + splicing from single-cell nanopore), LORALS (allele-specific long-read analysis). siRNA knockdown of 305 spliceosome/regulatory proteins reconstructed splicing-factor functional networks.
Splicing-code benchmark: spliceai (CNN, splice-site positions) and Pangolin (quantitative, multi-tissue) achieve highest accuracy among variant predictors, but performance is limited especially for exonic variants.

Connections

Methods companion to the wiki’s mechanism/code sources. Directly complements capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the on the splicing-code and spliceai/Pangolin (both flag exonic-variant and tissue-specific prediction as the weak spots; both name DeltaSplice, Borzoi, SpliceBERT, foundation models). This paper situates those models as the “decode” layer atop the sequencing/perturbation toolkit.
Provides the experimental backbone for the AS functionality debate: long-read isoform atlases reveal vast diversity, while CRISPR screens, ORF-interaction assays, proteomics (six-protease MS, ribosome profiling) and FAScore are the tactics for separating functional isoforms from mis-splicing noise (its closing “Do rare isoforms matter?” question).
post-transcriptional-splicing / cotranscriptional-splicing: long-read data show intron-removal order ≠ transcription order and coordinated distant-exon splicing; subcellular-fractionation long-read RNA-seq links isoforms to translational fate; ONT direct RNA couples isoform structure to m6A epitranscriptome.
splicing-and-disease: minigene/MPRA validation, sQTL/ASE genetics, CRISPR isogenic iPS-cell models (photoreceptors, cardiomyocytes); examples in NRXN1, NDUFB10, MAPT, and neurodevelopmental/neuropsychiatric risk via long-read neocortex isoform mapping.
splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins / splicing-regulatory-elements: SpliceRUSH and dCasRx-activator screens, the 305-factor siRNA network study, and RBFOX2 editing are the tools for dissecting regulator logic.

Open questions

Long-read platforms still need higher accuracy/throughput for complete cell-type-specific isoform annotation from low-input RNA; low-abundance transcripts remain low-precision and require validation.
Need integrated computational frameworks jointly modelling transcription initiation, splicing and 3’-end formation, and spatial methods at true isoform-level single-cell/subcellular resolution.
Splicing is highly dynamic, but time-resolved sequencing and methods to identify transient/partial splicing states are lacking.
Prediction goal unmet: a model that outputs the complete isoform repertoire and relative abundances per gene in any cell type/signalling context (likely needs foundation models integrating chromatin, RBP occupancy, RNA structure, epitranscriptome).
Moving from descriptive cataloguing to interpretable, mechanistic models of splicing control via large-scale perturbation + RNA-protein/structure/nuclear-organization mapping.
The core unresolved question: how to distinguish functional isoforms from pervasive splicing noise.

wiki/sources/wright-2022-alternative-spl.title: wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source
wiki/sources/wright-2022-alternative-spl.content:
Alternative splicing as a source of phenotypic diversity
One-line: A Perspective arguing that alternative-splicing is a dynamic, evolutionarily labile process that — despite mostly producing non-functional transcripts — can fuel adaptation, speciation and organismal complexity.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing generates multiple transcripts (and protein isoforms) per gene, expanding coding capacity and enabling quantitative regulation of protein levels; ~90–95% of human genes undergo AS.
The relative importance of AS event types varies by lineage: exon skipping dominates in metazoans, intron retention in plants and fungi. AS prevalence correlates with organism complexity (splicing-and-evolution).
The mode of spliceosome assembly shapes regulation: intron definition tends to give intron retention; exon definition (more prevalent in animals) gives exon skipping/inclusion and yields viable alternative protein-coding isoforms.
Most AS events are probably noise/errors and non-conserved, but a functional subset diversifies the proteome and is tied to development; coupled AS–NMD via splicing-and-disease-relevant poison exons drives autoregulation of splicing-factors.
Novel splice patterns arise via cis-effects (exon duplication, transition, exonization, intronization at splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements) and trans-effects (mutations in splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins); cis effects appear more common in interspecies divergence.
Divergent splicing underpins documented adaptations and contributes to splicing-and-evolution and speciation, often by producing non-functional truncated proteins rather than new functions.

Notable claims / data

An estimated 90–95% of human genes undergo AS; 88% of GWAS heritability-associated variants are in non-coding regions.
Long-read RNA-seq identified 7,874 complete transcript isoforms of Dscam1, thousands essential for neural circuit development.
~75% of human exon-skipping events seen by RNA-seq were also detected by ribosome profiling; ~40% of RNA-level splicing changes were concordant with translation changes in human glia/glioma.
Of one-third of human tissue-specific AS isoforms detected proteomically, 95% were highly conserved across vertebrates. Mutations in consensus splice sites account for ~10% of human inherited disease.
FOXP1 exons 18/18b: AS switch controls DNA-binding and pluripotency (OCT4, NANOG) vs differentiation; conserved across 46 vertebrate species (splicing-code).
Turtle temperature-dependent sex determination: CLK1/4 phosphorylates SR proteins (active <26°C → male; inactive >31°C → female), e.g. intron retention in chromatin modifier Jarid2 (splicing-and-chromatin).
Drosophila sex-determination cascade: Sxl → tra → dsx/fru/msl-2; dsx sex-specific splicing conserved across insects (Bombyx mori, Bactrocera oleae, Tribolium castaneum).
Stickleback MSX2A: a single exon-1 mutation creates a poly-G/ISE that activates an alternative 5′ splice site → frameshift + PTC → truncated protein → shorter spines in freshwater fish.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) TRPV1: trigeminal-specific exon 14a inclusion (PTC) yields a short isoform activating ~30°C, enabling infrared prey detection while full-length heat sensing is retained in dorsal root ganglia (~40% short transcripts in TG).
Capsella rubella selfing: two SNPs make CYP724A1 introns splice efficiently → more brassinosteroid → smaller petals (outbreeding→selfing transition).
Cavefish (Astyanax mexicanus) per2 exon-21 skipping (PTC) → truncated Per2 → enhanced lipogenesis via Pparγ; convergent in a second cavefish and Phreatichthys andruzzi.
TBXT (hominoid tail loss, preprint): Alu insertion in intron 6 forms a stem-loop driving ~50% exon-6 skipping; mouse heterozygotes lose tails; linked to neural tube defects.

Connections

alternative-splicing, splicing, spliceosome — core machinery and the central subject.
splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-code — weak splice sites of alternative exons, enhancers/silencers, and RBP-interpreted codes (rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors).
cotranscriptional-splicing — splicing is largely co-transcriptional; nascent-RNA data show long introns spliced before downstream exon transcription.
splicing-and-chromatin — chromatin marks/Pol II processivity affect AS; SR proteins regulate Jarid2 in turtle sex determination.
splicing-and-evolution, splicing-and-disease — divergent splicing, drift vs selection, and disease links (consensus-site mutations, poison exons, myelodysplasia).

Open questions

What fraction of AS events are genuinely functional vs noise, and how to distinguish them experimentally (CRISPR, long-read, single-cell)?
Relative contribution of cis vs trans regulatory mutations to AS evolution remains unclear.
Whether NMD acts as an evolutionary capacitor buffering AS variation needs experimental evidence.
How AS divergence relates causally to speciation and adaptation in most documented cases is still untested.
AS scaling with complexity rests on small species samples and needs broader validation across the tree of life.

wiki/syntheses/alternative-splicing-func.title: alternative-splicing-functionality-debate
wiki/syntheses/alternative-splicing-func.content:
Alternative Splicing: Functional or Noise? (Contradiction resolution)
Resolves the flag raised in overview and alternative-splicing:
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative argues most alternative-splicing
is functional, while wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source argues
most events are probably noise (with a conserved functional subset). Grounded
strictly in the ingested sources.
Verdict
Not a genuine factual contradiction — largely a difference in (1) the definition
of “functional”, (2) the denominator being counted, and (3) which evidence is
weighted. Both reviews actually agree on the central empirical points; they
disagree on how to classify the large tail of low-abundance, non-conserved
isoforms, which neither can yet settle with available data.
Where they actually AGREE

Prevalence: ~90–95% of human genes undergo AS
(wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source,
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative).
A conserved, functional core exists and drives development and physiology —
both accept this. Wright: of proteomically-detected tissue-specific isoforms,
95% are highly conserved across vertebrates; Marasco: highly conserved
neural microexons (SRRM4). Both treat conservation as evidence of function.
The same ribosome-profiling number: ~75% of (medium/high-abundance)
exon-skipping mRNAs are ribosome-engaged — this figure appears in both reviews
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source).
A low-abundance error tail exists: deep sequencing detects rare isoforms
that may be spliceosome errors (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative) —
Marasco doesn’t deny this; he denies it’s the majority of meaningful events.

Why the headline conclusions differ
1. Different definition of “functional”

Marasco ≈ reflected at the protein level / has a physiological role. He
emphasizes that mass-spec undercounts real isoforms (“absence of evidence is
not evidence of absence”) and that single-gene physiology (Dscam, CLK1 thermo-
sensing, PKM/Warburg, TRPV1, hTERT) shows pervasive function.
Wright ≈ encodes a preserved/selected protein function. Crucially, Wright
notes many adaptive AS events work by producing non-functional truncated
proteins (PTC-bearing) — stickleback MSX2A, cavefish per2, bat TRPV1.
So in Wright’s framing a transcript can be “non-functional” (at the protein
level) yet phenotypically and evolutionarily consequential. This dissolves
much of the apparent conflict: “non-functional protein” ≠ “no effect”.

2. Different denominator

Wright counts all RNA-seq-detected events, including the large pool of
low-abundance, non-conserved, lineage-specific variants → “most are noise.”
Marasco focuses on medium/high-abundance events and conserved switches →
“most [that matter] are functional.” They are describing different slices of the
same distribution.

3. Different evidence weighting

Marasco leans on ribosome profiling + the insensitivity of proteomics.
Wright leans on cross-species conservation as the functionality filter, and
treats AS as an evolutionarily labile, mostly-drift process from which
selection occasionally recruits useful variants.

Synthesised position
A defensible reconciliation consistent with all three sources:

~90–95% of human genes undergo AS, but isoform output is a steep
abundance/conservation distribution.
The head (abundant, conserved, often tissue-specific) is largely
functional — and both reviews agree here.
The long tail (rare, non-conserved) is dominated by stochastic
spliceosome noise/error — Wright’s emphasis, compatible with Lee’s error caveat.
“Functional” must be split into senses: translated stable protein /
conserved-selected / quantitative regulator via AS–NMD
poison exons (even untranslated isoforms regulate protein level — both accept).
Disagreement shrinks once the sense is fixed.

Residual genuine uncertainty
The real open question — what fraction of the tail is functional — is
unresolved because current tools can’t settle it. Both reviews propose the same
fixes: long-read sequencing, single-cell, and CRISPR perturbation to test
isoforms directly (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source). Until then this is a
difference of emphasis over an unmeasured tail, not a contradiction of fact.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — “mostly functional”; proteomics
undercounts; ribosome profiling; physiological case studies; long-read proposal
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — “mostly noise + conserved
subset”; conservation as filter; adaptation via non-functional truncated proteins
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — low-abundance isoforms may
be spliceosome errors (the error-tail caveat)

wiki/syntheses/cotranscriptional-vs-post.title: cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing
wiki/syntheses/cotranscriptional-vs-post.content:
Co-transcriptional vs Post-transcriptional Splicing
A comparison of the two timing modes of intron removal. Grounded in the locally
ingested sources. See the concept pages cotranscriptional-splicing and
post-transcriptional-splicing.
The core distinction
Both modes use the same spliceosome and the same assembly steps
(E → A → B → Bact/B* → C); what differs is when splicing happens relative to
transcription and 3′-end processing
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).

Co-transcriptional — intron removed during synthesis, while the nascent
pre-mRNA is still tethered to elongating RNA Pol II.
Post-transcriptional — removal completes after transcription and
polyadenylation, typically while the polyadenylated transcript is still
chromatin-associated.

Side-by-side


















































AxisCo-transcriptionalPost-transcriptionalTimingDuring transcription, on nascent RNAAfter transcription + polyadenylationLocationElongating Pol II / chromatinChromatin-associated (poly(A)+ RNA), nuclear specklesExtent~70% of mammalian introns excised on chromatin (much co-transcriptional)~27–45% of introns (up to ~40% retained after termination); >70% of genes have ≥1SpliceosomeIdentical assembly stepsIdentical assembly steps (only timing differs)Primary control knobPol II elongation rate (kinetic coupling) + chromatincis features (GC, length, ss strength, RNA structure) + trans factors + modificationsMechanistic model”Window of opportunity”: slow Pol II favors inclusion of class I / skipping of class II exonsDelay buys time for splice-site choice; splicing completion gates transcript releaseFunctional consequenceSets alternative-splicing outcomes via kineticsGates nuclear export & translation; regulatory reservoir (detained introns)Key sourcesmarasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternativechoquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of
What drives each

Co-transcriptional → kinetic coupling. Splice-site choice depends on
cis-splicing-regulatory-elements, trans splicing-factors, and Pol II
elongation rate (~3–4 kb/min). A slow polymerase widens the window for an
upstream splice site to be recognized before a competitor
appears — slow elongation promotes class I exon inclusion and class II exon
skipping. Elongation is itself tuned by chromatin
(histone marks, nucleosomes) and the Pol II CTD
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).
Post-transcriptional → delayed, gated removal. Timing is set by cis
features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary
structure) and trans factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins, U2
snRNA, SNIP1), modulated by CLK/SRPK phosphorylation, PRMT methylation and m6A.
Detained introns are a specialized held-until-signaled class (neurons,
spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation, heat shock)
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).

How they relate (not a strict binary)
The two are endpoints of a timing continuum, not mutually exclusive
categories — a single transcript’s introns can be removed at different times
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of). Notably, introns flanking
alternative exons are preferentially post-transcriptional (67% vs 30% for
constitutive introns in mESCs), plausibly buying time for correct splice-site
choice — so the post-transcriptional delay is itself a regulatory device feeding
alternative-splicing (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Supporting data (post-transcriptional)

Median chromatin half-life of mRNA ~50 min in K562 (some genes >170 min),
often exceeding transcription time itself.
A β-globin reporter intron was excised ~10× faster and more often (87%) after
release from the transcription site; for 6 of 10 endogenous human introns
post-transcriptional excision was more probable than co-transcriptional.
Most retained introns are eventually spliced productively; nuclear decay is the
minority fate (~4% of genes, “PUND” transcripts).
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)

Open questions

What triggers the eventual post-transcriptional reaction, and does the delay
occur before or after spliceosome engagement?
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)
Do exons regulated by transcription rate (co-transcriptional kinetics) overlap
with those regulated by delayed removal of flanking introns?
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)

Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — primary: extent, kinetics,
detained introns, release gating, determinants of post-transcriptional splicing
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — kinetic coupling model;
class I/II exons; elongation–chromatin link
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — identical assembly
steps; Pol II ~3–4 kb/min; slow-polymerase effects

wiki/topics/splicing.title: splicing
wiki/topics/splicing.content:
Splicing
Topic hub for pre-mRNA splicing and alternative splicing — the removal
of introns from precursor mRNA by the spliceosome and its regulation, which
together form a central layer of eukaryotic gene regulation. This page curates
the sources, the concept map, and the current synthesis across them.
Why it matters
Splicing converts intron-containing “mosaic” genes into mature mRNA via a
two-step transesterification reaction. alternative-splicing — the regulated
inclusion/exclusion of exons — lets one gene produce many isoforms, expanding
the proteome and underpinning organismal complexity, development, disease, and
therapy (see editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer).
Sources (7)























































SourceYearAnglelee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative2015Spliceosome biochemistry/structure; the spliceosome as a ribozyme; mechanisms of AS regulation (foundational)marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative2022Physiology of AS — how isoform diversity shapes tissues, thermal regulation, cancer, ASDwright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source2022Evolution — AS as a labile source of phenotypic diversity, adaptation, speciationrogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing2022Regulation mechanisms + disease + first splicing-modulating therapiescapitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the2024The splicing-code — computational models from PWMs to deep learningchoquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of2025post-transcriptional-splicing — splicing timing as a regulatory layereditorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer2025Framing editorial / orientation to the whole collectionsousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying2026splicing-methods — experimental & computational toolkit (long-read, CRISPR, sQTL, deep learning)peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes2026rna-structure-and-splicing — RNA G-quadruplexes as structural regulators of AS (Trends in Genetics)
Concept map

Machinery & chemistry — spliceosome · splicing · splice-sites
Regulation — splicing-regulatory-elements · splicing-factors · rna-binding-proteins · splicing-code · rna-structure-and-splicing
Timing & coupling — cotranscriptional-splicing · post-transcriptional-splicing · splicing-and-chromatin
Outcomes — alternative-splicing · splicing-and-disease · splicing-therapeutics · splicing-and-evolution
How we study it — splicing-methods

Key entities

Factors — sf3b1 · u2af1 · srsf1 · hnrnpa1 · ptbp1
Gene examples — dscam · smn2
Structures — g-quadruplex
Tools — spliceai

Syntheses

cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate

Current synthesis (across the 7 sources)

The machine is settled; the regulation is the frontier. The spliceosome
is a ~4.8 MDa dynamic RNP of five snRNPs whose catalytic core is RNA — a
ribozyme related to self-splicing group II introns (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).
Cryo-EM has since resolved most assembly/catalytic states
(rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). The open questions are
about control, not chemistry.
A position-dependent code. Weak, degenerate metazoan splice-sites plus
exonic/intronic enhancers and silencers (splicing-regulatory-elements) read
by SR proteins and hnRNPs (splicing-factors) determine outcomes. Modeling
this splicing-code has gone from position weight matrices to deep-learning
models (SpliceAI, Pangolin) that now call ~10% of pathogenic rare-disease
variants (capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).
Timing is itself regulatory. Splicing is largely co-transcriptional, but up
to ~40% of mammalian introns are spliced post-transcriptionally while
transcripts remain chromatin-associated — gating export and translation
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of, cotranscriptional-splicing).
Most AS may be noise, but a conserved subset is functional and adaptive.
Proteomics-vs-ribosome-profiling debate on functionality
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative vs
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source); AS diverges faster than
expression and can drive adaptation (splicing-and-evolution).
Mechanism → medicine. Splicing-factor mutations drive cancer (SF3B1,
U2AF1) and genetic disease; the first splicing therapies are approved
(nusinersen, risdiplam for SMA; eteplirsen for DMD) — see
splicing-therapeutics and splicing-and-disease.

Open questions

What triggers the co- vs post-transcriptional timing switch? (mechanism unknown — choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)
How much alternative splicing is functional vs noise? (unresolved — marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative / wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source)
Can splicing-code models become interpretable and tissue-specific? (capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the)


Note: claims above are grounded in the locally ingested sources only. See each
source page for citations.
